  - The image model is a **product**, not a sum — that is precisely why we need the log.
- **KTU Frequently Asked Variations:** Numerical problems on $H(u,v)$ values at specific $D$ values are common in 3-mark and 7-mark questions.
- **Practical Tip for Parameter Selection:** Start with $\gamma_L = 0.4$, $\gamma_H = 1.5$, $D_0 =$ image size/10, $c = 1$, then tune.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Image Degradation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT604 - MEDICAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Image Degradation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Image Degradation** is the process by which an original, pristine image loses its quality, clarity, or fidelity due to imperfections in the image acquisition system, transmission medium, or environmental conditions. In the context of **Medical Image Processing**, degradation refers to the corruption introduced during imaging modalities such as X-ray, CT, MRI, or Ultrasound due to physical limitations of sensors, patient motion, scattering, and electronic noise.

Mathematically, the **degradation model** is expressed as:

$$g(x, y) = H[f(x, y)] + \eta(x, y)$$

Where:
- $f(x, y)$ = Original (input) image
- $g(x, y)$ = Degraded (observed) image
- $H$ = Degradation operator (a linear, position-invariant system in most KTU contexts)
- $\eta(x, y)$ = Additive noise term

If the system $H$ is **linear and shift-invariant (LSI)**, the spatial domain representation becomes a **convolution**:

$$g(x, y) = f(x, y) * h(x, y) + \eta(x, y)$$

In the **frequency domain**, by the Convolution Theorem, this elegantly transforms to:

$$G(u, v) = H(u, v) \cdot F(u, v) + N(u, v)$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Smudged Window:**
> Imagine you are looking at a beautiful painting through a slightly dirty window. The painting is the **original image $f(x,y)$**. The dirty glass with smudges and fingerprints represents the **degradation function $H$** — it blurs and distorts what you see. The dust particles and tiny scratches on the glass represent the **noise $\eta(x,y)$**. The picture your eyes actually perceive (smudged + dusty version) is the **degraded image $g(x,y)$**. 
>
> **Image Restoration** (the next module) is essentially the act of "cleaning the window" — figuring out what the original painting looked like from what you can see.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** | Measured in **dB** | Quantifies noise level |
| **Peak SNR (PSNR)** | Measured in **dB** | Image fidelity measure |
| **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** | Pixel intensity² units | Average squared pixel error |
| **Sampling Rate (Nyquist)** | $\geq 2 \times f_{max}$ | Aliasing prevention |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 PBEBT604 syllabus (Module 3), students must master the spatial and frequency domain models of degradation, classification of noise distributions, and methods to estimate the degradation function for medical images (CT, MRI, Ultrasound, X-Ray).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Degradation Process — Step-by-Step Logic

The complete degradation pipeline in medical imaging follows this logical sequence:

1. **Image Formation Stage** — A continuous physical signal (e.g., X-ray photons passing through tissue) is captured.
2. **Sensor Response Stage** — The sensor introduces **point-spread function (PSF)** blurring.
3. **Motion Blur Stage** — Patient breathing, cardiac motion, or sensor vibration induces **motion blur**.
4. **Electronic Noise Stage** — Thermal noise, shot noise, and quantization noise are **additively** injected.
5. **Atmospheric/Medium Stage** — In some modalities (ultrasound), the medium itself scatters the signal.
6. **Display/Storage Stage** — Quantization and compression may further degrade the image.

## 2.2 Degradation Function Categories

### (A) Atmospheric / Medium Turbulence (less relevant to medical, mentioned for completeness)
$$H(u, v) = e^{-k\left(u^2 + v^2\right)^{5/6}}$$

### (B) Motion Blur (highly relevant to medical imaging — patient motion)
$$H(u, v) = \frac{T}{\pi(ua + vb)} \sin\left[\pi(ua + vb)\right] e^{-j\pi(ua + vb)}$$

Where $T$ = exposure time, $(a, b)$ = velocity components of motion.

### (C) Out-of-Focus Blur (relevant in microscopy and X-ray)
$$H(u, v) = \frac{J_1(ar)}{ar}$$

Where $J_1$ is the **Bessel function of the first kind**, order 1, and $r = \sqrt{u^2 + v^2}$.

## 2.3 Noise Models — The Heart of Degradation

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Noise Models Matter:** In medical imaging, accurate noise modeling is **critical** because reconstruction algorithms (Filtered Back-Projection, Iterative Reconstruction) require statistical noise assumptions to produce diagnostic-quality images.

### **KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet**

| Noise Type | PDF Formula $p(z)$ | Mean $\mu$ | Variance $\sigma^2$ | Medical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Gaussian** | $p(z) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\left(-\dfrac{(z-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | $\mu$ | $\sigma^2$ | Thermal/electronic noise in MRI, CT detectors |
| **Rayleigh** | $p(z) = \dfrac{2}{b}(z-a)\exp\left(-\dfrac{(z-a)^2}{b}\right)$ for $z \geq a$ | $a + \sqrt{\pi b/4}$ | $\dfrac{b(4-\pi)}{4}$ | Speckle-related distributions |
| **Erlang (Gamma)** | $p(z) = \dfrac{a^b z^{b-1}}{(b-1)!} e^{-az}$ for $z \geq 0$ | $\dfrac{b}{a}$ | $\dfrac{b}{a^2}$ | Laser imaging, certain X-ray systems |
| **Exponential** | $p(z) = a\,e^{-az}$ for $z \geq 0$ | $\dfrac{1}{a}$ | $\dfrac{1}{a^2}$ | Limit case of Erlang with $b=1$ |
| **Uniform** | $p(z) = \dfrac{1}{b-a}$ for $a \leq z \leq b$ | $\dfrac{a+b}{2}$ | $\dfrac{(b-a)^2}{12}$ | Quantization noise approximation |
| **Salt & Pepper (Impulse)** | $p(z) = P_s$ for $z=0$, $P_p$ for $z=2^k-1$ | — | — | Dead pixels, transmission errors, faulty sensors |
| **Poisson (Shot)** | $p(z) = \dfrac{\lambda^z e^{-\lambda}}{z!}$ | $\lambda$ | $\lambda$ | X-ray photon counting, low-dose CT |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Poisson Noise:** In X-ray and nuclear medicine imaging, the **photon-counting nature** of the imaging process means noise follows a **Poisson distribution**. When the mean count $\lambda$ is large (>30), Poisson $\approx$ Gaussian, which is a key assumption in many reconstruction algorithms.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CT Scanner Design:** Engineers use Gaussian + Poisson noise models to calibrate detector sensitivity.
- **MRI Reconstruction:** Noise is predominantly **Rician** (a variant of Rayleigh) due to complex k-space data; this informs filter design.
- **Ultrasound Imaging:** Speckle noise is a multiplicative phenomenon requiring specialized **homomorphic filtering** or **wavelet denoising**.
- **PET/SPECT:** Poisson statistics govern the reconstruction, leading to algorithms like **Maximum Likelihood Expectation Maximization (MLEM)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Spatial Degradation to Frequency Domain

We begin with the spatial convolution model and transform it into the frequency domain using the **Fourier Transform**. This is a **mandatory derivation** for KTU 14-mark questions.

### **Step 1: Start with the Spatial Domain Degradation Equation**

$$g(x, y) = f(x, y) * h(x, y) + \eta(x, y)$$

> **Logic:** The degraded image equals the convolution of the original image with the degradation PSF, plus additive noise.

### **Step 2: Apply the Fourier Transform to Both Sides**

Taking $\mathcal{F}\{\cdot\}$ of both sides:

$$\mathcal{F}\{g(x, y)\} = \mathcal{F}\{f(x, y) * h(x, y)\} + \mathcal{F}\{\eta(x, y)\}$$

### **Step 3: Apply the Convolution Theorem**

By the **Convolution Theorem**, $\mathcal{F}\{f * h\} = F \cdot H$:

$$G(u, v) = F(u, v) \cdot H(u, v) + N(u, v)$$

> **Logic:** Convolution in the spatial domain becomes point-wise multiplication in the frequency domain. This is **mathematically elegant** and computationally efficient (FFT).

### **Step 4: Express the Original Image in Terms of the Degraded**

Rearranging for the original image spectrum:

$$F(u, v) = \frac{G(u, v) - N(u, v)}{H(u, v)}$$

> **Logic:** This sets the stage for **image restoration** (Wiener filtering, inverse filtering) in the next module.

## 3.2 Derivation: Mean and Variance of the Gaussian Noise PDF

Starting with the Gaussian PDF:

$$p(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\left(-\frac{(z-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

### **Step 1: Compute the Mean $\mu$**

The mean is defined as:

$$\mu = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} z \cdot p(z) \, dz$$

Substituting:

$$\mu = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} z \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\left(-\frac{(z-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) dz$$

By symmetry of the Gaussian about its center, this integral evaluates to $\mu$.

$$\boxed{\mu = \mu}$$

> **Logic:** The mean parameter $\mu$ in the PDF is **self-consistent** — the distribution is centered at $\mu$ by construction.

### **Step 2: Compute the Variance $\sigma^2$**

$$\sigma^2 = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (z - \mu)^2 \cdot p(z) \, dz$$

Let $w = z - \mu$, so $dz = dw$:

$$\sigma^2 = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} w^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\left(-\frac{w^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) dw$$

Using the standard Gaussian integral identity $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} w^2 e^{-w^2/2\sigma^2} dw = \sigma\sqrt{2\pi} \cdot \sigma^2$:

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \cdot \sigma\sqrt{2\pi} \cdot \sigma^2$$

$$\boxed{\sigma^2 = \sigma^2}$$

> **Logic:** The variance parameter in the PDF matches the computed statistical variance — a fundamental property of normal distributions.

## 3.3 Derivation: PSNR and MSE

These are **evaluation metrics** for any restoration algorithm that must be referenced when discussing degradation.

### **Step 1: Mean Squared Error (MSE)**

$$MSE = \frac{1}{MN} \sum_{i=0}^{M-1} \sum_{j=0}^{N-1} \left[f(i,j) - \hat{f}(i,j)\right]^2$$

Where $f(i,j)$ is the original, $\hat{f}(i,j)$ is the restored image, and $M \times N$ is the image size.

### **Step 2: Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR)**

$$PSNR = 10 \log_{10} \left(\frac{L^2}{MSE}\right) \text{ dB}$$

> **Logic:** $L$ is the maximum possible pixel value (e.g., 255 for 8-bit images). Higher PSNR = better restoration quality.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Noise Behaviors

| Noise Type | Additive? | Multiplicative? | Spatial/Frequency Domain? | Dominant in |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gaussian | ✓ | ✗ | Both, often frequency-flat | MRI, electronic noise |
| Salt & Pepper | ✓ | ✗ | Spatial (impulsive) | Faulty sensors, transmission |
| Poisson (Shot) | ✗ (signal-dependent) | ✗ | Spatial (photon statistics) | X-ray, PET, low-dose CT |
| Speckle | ✗ | ✓ | Both, multiplicative | Ultrasound, SAR, OCT |
| Periodic | ✓ | ✗ | **Frequency domain (spikes)** | Electrical interference, 50/60 Hz mains |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: The Complete Degradation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original Medical Image f x y] --> B[Scene/Sensor Interaction]
    B --> C[Atmospheric or Medium Attenuation H]
    C --> D[Motion Blur Operator]
    D --> E[Optical System PSF]
    E --> F[Sensor Sampling and Quantization]
    F --> G[Electronic Noise Source eta x y]
    G --> H[Degraded Image g x y]
    H --> I[Storage or Transmission Channel]
    I --> J[Final Observed Image]
    J --> K[Image Restoration Module 4]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Each block represents a potential source of degradation. In a CT scanner, for instance, blocks C and E dominate, while in MRI, block G (Gaussian/Rician noise) is most prominent.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Noise Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    N[Image Noise] --> N1[Additive Noise]
    N --> N2[Multiplicative Noise]
    N1 --> N1A[Gaussian]
    N1 --> N1B[Salt and Pepper]
    N1 --> N1C[Uniform Quantization]
    N1 --> N1D[Periodic 50 or 60 Hz]
    N2 --> N2A[Speckle]
    N2 --> N2B[Poisson Signal Dependent]
    N2A --> M[Ultrasound and OCT]
    N2B --> P[X-ray and PET]
    N1A --> Q[MRI and CT]
    N1B --> R[Faulty Detector Arrays]
```

## 4.3 Flow Diagram: Estimation of Degradation Function

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step 1 Image Observation] --> B[Step 2 Prior Knowledge of H]
    B --> C[Step 3 Method Selection]
    C --> D1[Method A Experimentation]
    C --> D2[Method B Modeling]
    C --> D3[Method C Blind Estimation]
    D1 --> E[Estimate H u v]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Apply to Restoration Filter]
```

> **Explanation of the Estimation Methods:**
> - **Method A (Experimentation):** Image a known target (e.g., calibration phantom), observe the output, compute $H(u,v) = G(u,v)/F(u,v)$.
> - **Method B (Mathematical Modeling):** Use physical models (motion blur formula, atmospheric turbulence).
> - **Method C (Blind Estimation):** Use algorithms like **Maximum Likelihood** or **Bayesian inference** to estimate $H$ from the degraded image alone.

## 4.4 Diagram: Frequency Domain Signatures of Noise

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Gaussian Noise] --> A1[Flat Frequency Spectrum]
    B[Salt and Pepper Noise] --> B1[Broad Spectrum, High Frequency]
    C[Periodic Noise] --> C1[Discrete Spikes in Frequency]
    D[Speckle Noise] --> D1[Modulates Image Spectrum]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Image Degradation. List the major sources of noise in medical imaging.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Image degradation is the process by which an image loses quality during acquisition, transmission, or storage, modeled mathematically as $g(x,y) = f(x,y) * h(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$.

**Major sources of noise in medical imaging:**
1. **Thermal/electronic noise** (Gaussian) — dominant in MRI, CT detectors.
2. **Shot/Photon noise** (Poisson) — dominant in X-ray, PET, SPECT.
3. **Speckle noise** (multiplicative) — dominant in Ultrasound, OCT.
4. **Quantization noise** (Uniform) — from A/D conversion.
5. **Patient motion artifacts** — respiratory, cardiac motion.
6. **Salt & Pepper noise** — faulty detector elements or transmission errors.

### **Question 2: Distinguish between additive and multiplicative noise with examples.**
`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Additive Noise | Multiplicative Noise |
|---|---|---|
| **Equation** | $g(x,y) = f(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$ | $g(x,y) = f(x,y) \cdot \eta(x,y)$ |
| **Independence** | Independent of signal | Signal-dependent |
| **Homomorphic transform** | Not required | Converted to additive via $\log$ |
| **Example 1** | Gaussian noise (thermal) | Speckle noise (Ultrasound) |
| **Example 2** | Salt & Pepper (transmission) | Poisson noise (X-ray, low count) |
| **Removal** | Spatial/Frequency filters | Homomorphic, wavelet filters |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 3 Internal Choice A]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Derive the frequency domain representation of the image degradation model and explain each term.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** The spatial domain model is:
$$g(x,y) = f(x,y) * h(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$$

**Step 2:** Apply the 2D Discrete Fourier Transform:
$$G(u,v) = F(u,v) H(u,v) + N(u,v)$$

**Explanation of Terms:**
- $G(u,v)$ — Fourier transform of the degraded image (what we observe).
- $F(u,v)$ — Fourier transform of the original image (what we want to recover).
- $H(u,v)$ — Degradation transfer function; the **optical transfer function (OTF)** or modulation transfer function (MTF) in medical systems.
- $N(u,v)$ — Fourier transform of the additive noise.

**Step 3:** To recover the original image spectrum (assuming $N \approx 0$):
$$F(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

> **Why this matters:** This is the foundation of **inverse filtering**. When $H(u,v) \to 0$, noise gets amplified, motivating **Wiener filtering**.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: With relevant equations and PDF plots, explain Gaussian, Salt & Pepper, and Poisson noise models. State the medical modality where each is dominant.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Gaussian Noise PDF:**
$$p(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\left(-\frac{(z-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$
- **Properties:** Bell-shaped, symmetric, governed by $\mu$ and $\sigma^2$.
- **Dominant in:** MRI (thermal noise in receiver coils), CT detector electronics.

**2. Salt & Pepper (Impulse) Noise PDF:**
$$p(z) = \begin{cases} P_s & \text{for } z = 0 \text{ (pepper)} \\ P_p & \text{for } z = 2^k - 1 \text{ (salt)} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$
- **Properties:** Appears as random black/white pixels.
- **Dominant in:** Faulty detector arrays, transmission bit errors, corrupted scanlines.

**3. Poisson (Shot) Noise PDF:**
$$p(z) = \frac{\lambda^z e^{-\lambda}}{z!}, \quad z = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$
- **Properties:** Discrete distribution, mean = variance = $\lambda$.
- **Dominant in:** X-ray imaging, PET, SPECT, low-dose CT (photon counting).

> **Synthesis:** The choice of restoration filter depends critically on the noise type. **Median filter** for salt & pepper, **Wiener filter** for Gaussian, and **Poisson-MLEM algorithms** for Poisson noise.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

`[KTU University Exam - June 2024 - Module 3 Internal Choice B]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Explain the three principal methods for estimating the degradation function $H(u,v)$ in medical imaging. Use a calibration phantom example.**

**Model Solution:**

**Method 1: Image Observation (Experimentation)**
- Reconstruct a known test object (e.g., **line pair phantom**, **CT resolution phantom**).
- Acquire the image $g(x,y)$ and compute $G(u,v) = \mathcal{F}\{g(x,y)\}$.
- Knowing the original $F(u,v)$, estimate:
$$H(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{F(u,v)}$$
- **Used in:** CT and MRI acceptance testing.

**Method 2: Mathematical Modeling**
- Use physical models: motion blur, out-of-focus blur, atmospheric turbulence.
- Example: Motion blur model:
$$H(u,v) = \frac{T}{\pi(ua+vb)} \sin[\pi(ua+vb)] e^{-j\pi(ua+vb)}$$
- **Used in:** Correcting known motion patterns (e.g., respiratory gating).

**Method 3: Blind Deconvolution / Estimation**
- Estimate $H$ **without** prior knowledge of the original.
- Uses **Maximum Likelihood (ML)**, **Expectation Maximization (EM)**, or **Bayesian inference**.
- **Used in:** Astronomical and microscopy imaging where calibration is impractical.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: A 256×256 8-bit medical image is corrupted by Gaussian noise with $\sigma = 20$. The original image has $f(i,j) = 100$ (constant). Compute the MSE and PSNR. Show all steps.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Image size: $M \times N = 256 \times 256$
- Bit depth: 8-bit, so $L = 2^8 - 1 = 255$
- Original pixel: $f(i,j) = 100$
- Noise standard deviation: $\sigma = 20$

**Step 1: Compute the Degraded Image**
$$g(i,j) = f(i,j) + \eta(i,j) = 100 + \eta(i,j)$$

The noise $\eta$ is Gaussian with $\mu = 0$, $\sigma = 20$.

**Step 2: Compute the MSE**

The expected squared error is $E[(f - g)^2] = E[\eta^2] = \sigma^2 = 400$.

$$MSE = \frac{1}{MN} \sum_{i=0}^{255} \sum_{j=0}^{255} \eta^2(i,j) = \sigma^2 = 400$$

**Step 3: Compute the PSNR**

$$PSNR = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{L^2}{MSE}\right)$$

$$PSNR = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{255^2}{400}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{65025}{400}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(162.5625)$$

$$PSNR = 10 \times 2.2111 \approx 22.11 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4: Interpretation**
A PSNR of **22.11 dB** indicates **significant noise contamination** — the image is visibly degraded. Restoration algorithms should aim to boost PSNR to > 30 dB for diagnostic acceptability.

$$\boxed{PSNR \approx 22.11 \text{ dB}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing noise types in medical modalities:** Many students write "Poisson noise is Gaussian" — this is **only approximate** at high counts. Always state the assumption.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the PSF vs. OTF distinction:** PSF is in **spatial domain**; OTF/MTF is in **frequency domain**. Mixing them up loses 2-3 marks.
> 
> 3. **Skipping steps in PSNR derivation:** Examiners require **explicit step-by-step** calculation. A direct answer of "22.11 dB" without intermediate steps gets **partial credit only**.
> 
> 4. **Missing the constant $\mu$ in Gaussian PDF:** When writing the PDF, forgetting $\mu$ in the exponent $(z-\mu)^2$ is a common slip.
> 
> 5. **Treating speckle as additive noise:** Ultrasound speckle is **multiplicative** — students who apply Gaussian denoising methods lose marks.
> 
> 6. **Not mentioning the convolution theorem by name:** When deriving the frequency domain model, always **explicitly name the Convolution Theorem** for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**
> 
> - **Degradation Equation:** $g(x,y) = f(x,y) * h(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$
> - **Frequency Domain:** $G(u,v) = H(u,v)F(u,v) + N(u,v)$
> - **Restoration Foundation:** $F(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v) - N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$
> - **Gaussian Noise:** Bell-shaped PDF; thermal noise in MRI/CT.
> - **Poisson Noise:** Mean = Variance = $\lambda$; photon-counting in X-ray/PET.
> - **Salt & Pepper:** Impulsive; black/white pixel corruption; fixed sensor elements.
> - **Speckle Noise:** **Multiplicative**; dominant in Ultrasound/OCT.
> - **PSNR Formula:** $PSNR = 10\log_{10}(L^2/MSE)$ dB
> - **MSE Formula:** $MSE = \frac{1}{MN}\sum\sum[f(i,j) - \hat{f}(i,j)]^2$
> - **Motion Blur:** Function of exposure time $T$ and velocity $(a,b)$.
> - **Convolution Theorem:** Spatial convolution ↔ Frequency multiplication.
> - **Estimation Methods:** Observation, Modeling, Blind estimation.
> - **Medical Modality ↔ Noise Mapping:**
>   - X-ray → Poisson
>   - CT → Gaussian + Poisson
>   - MRI → Gaussian (Rician in magnitude)
>   - Ultrasound → Speckle (multiplicative)
>   - PET/SPECT → Poisson
> - **Key Constants:** $L = 255$ for 8-bit images; Nyquist rate $= 2f_{max}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT604 - MEDICAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Noise

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Medical Image Processing**, **noise** is formally defined as an unwanted, random perturbation or stochastic variation in image pixel intensity values that corrupts the true underlying signal of the anatomical or pathological structure being imaged. Mathematically, a noise-corrupted image is modeled as:

$$g(x, y) = f(x, y) + \eta(x, y)$$

where:
- $f(x, y)$ is the original, noise-free image (the true signal),
- $\eta(x, y)$ is the additive noise component,
- $g(x, y)$ is the observed (degraded) image.

In many medical imaging modalities, the noise is **signal-dependent (multiplicative)**:

$$g(x, y) = f(x, y) \cdot \eta(x, y)$$

A more general formulation combines both:

$$g(x, y) = f(x, y) + f(x, y) \cdot \eta(x, y) = f(x, y)[1 + \eta(x, y)]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Noise is the **fundamental limiting factor** in all medical imaging systems (X-ray, CT, MRI, Ultrasound, PET). It determines the **low-contrast detectability** of lesions, governs the **dose-vs-quality trade-off** in ionizing imaging, and directly impacts the diagnostic confidence of radiologists.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Whispering Classroom"

Imagine a quiet classroom where a teacher (the true signal $f$) is dictating a clear message. Now imagine dozens of students whispering random things in the background (the noise $\eta$). The closer you sit to the teacher, the clearer the message (high **SNR**). The farther you sit, the more the whispers drown out the message (low **SNR**). 

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Map:**
> - **Signal (Teacher's voice)** = Real anatomical structure (e.g., a tumor, bone edge, organ boundary).
> - **Noise (Student whispers)** = Random electronic fluctuations, photon-counting statistics, thermal motion of electrons.
> - **SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio)** = How clearly the message reaches you.
> - **Filtering** = Asking the noisy students to be quiet so you can hear clearly.

## 1.3 Why Noise is Critical in Medical Imaging

| Medical Modality | Dominant Noise Source | Typical Visual Appearance |
|---|---|---|
| **X-Ray / CT** | Quantum (Poisson) mottle + electronic noise | Grainy, salt-and-pepper speckle |
| **MRI** | Thermal (Johnson-Nyquist) noise + Rician | Fine random grain, Rician bias in low-SNR |
| **Ultrasound** | Speckle (multiplicative, coherent interference) | Granular bright/dark pattern |
| **PET / SPECT** | Poisson (count-starved) + scatter | High-frequency salt-and-pepper |
| **Digital Mammography** | Quantum + structured noise | Microcalcifications obscured by grain |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric — SNR:** The universal figure of merit is the **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**, defined as:
> $$\text{SNR} = \frac{\mu_{\text{signal}}}{\sigma_{\text{noise}}}$$
> where $\mu_{\text{signal}}$ is the mean intensity of the region of interest and $\sigma_{\text{noise}}$ is the standard deviation of the noise. In dB: $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(\mu / \sigma)$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sources of Noise in Medical Imaging Systems

The generation of noise in any imaging chain can be traced to two fundamental physical origins:

### A. Quantum (Photon) Noise — The Statistical Nature of Light
- Photons arriving at the detector follow a **Poisson distribution** because they are discrete, random events.
- If $N$ photons are expected, the standard deviation of the count is $\sqrt{N}$.
- **Relative noise** $= 1/\sqrt{N}$. Hence, **higher dose $\Rightarrow$ lower relative noise**.

### B. Electronic / Thermal Noise
- Caused by thermal agitation of electrons in detector electronics and amplifier circuits.
- Modeled as **Gaussian (additive white) noise** with zero mean.
- Independent of signal intensity.

## 2.2 Mathematical Noise Models — The Six Canonical Types

### Type 1: Gaussian Noise (Electronic / Thermal)
The pixel intensity distribution follows the normal distribution:

$$p(z) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left[-\frac{(z - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right]$$

- $\mu$ = mean (often 0),
- $\sigma^2$ = variance.
- ~70% of values lie within $[\mu - \sigma, \mu + \sigma]$.

### Type 2: Uniform Noise
- Used in **random number generators**, quantization baseline, and simulation.
- $$p(z) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{b-a}, & a \le z \le b \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$
- Mean $= (a+b)/2$, Variance $= (b-a)^2/12$.

### Type 3: Salt-and-Pepper Noise (Impulse Noise)
- Appears as **random black and white pixels** (like salt grains and pepper grains scattered on a surface).
- Caused by **bit errors** during transmission, faulty memory cells, or dead detector elements.
- PDF: $P_a$ probability of pepper (dark), $P_b$ probability of salt (bright); otherwise unchanged.

### Type 4: Poisson (Shot / Quantum) Noise
- Arises because photon detection is a counting process.
- For mean $\lambda$: $\displaystyle P(k) = \frac{\lambda^k e^{-\lambda}}{k!}$
- Mean $= \lambda$, Variance $= \lambda$ (a defining property).
- For high $\lambda$, Poisson $\to$ Gaussian by Central Limit Theorem.

### Type 5: Speckle Noise (Multiplicative)
- **The signature noise of coherent imaging** (Ultrasound, SAR, OCT).
- Modeled as: $g(x,y) = f(x,y) \cdot u(x,y)$ where $u$ has mean 1 and variance $\sigma_u^2$.
- PDF in fully developed speckle (single-look SAR): Rayleigh; in intensity: Exponential.

### Type 6: Periodic / Structured Noise
- Arises from **electromagnetic interference**, mains hum (50/60 Hz pickup), or mechanical vibration.
- Appears as sinusoidal patterns in the 2D Fourier domain (concentrated peaks).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Noise Type | PDF / Formula | Mean | Variance | Key Parameter |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Gaussian** | $\frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-(z-\mu)^2/2\sigma^2}$ | $\mu$ | $\sigma^2$ | $\sigma$ controls spread |
| 2 | **Uniform** | $1/(b-a)$ for $z \in [a,b]$ | $(a+b)/2$ | $(b-a)^2/12$ | Range $[a,b]$ |
| 3 | **Salt & Pepper** | $P_a$ at 0, $P_b$ at $2^k-1$ | — | — | Probability $P_a, P_b$ |
| 4 | **Poisson (Shot)** | $\frac{\lambda^k e^{-\lambda}}{k!}$ | $\lambda$ | $\lambda$ | Mean $=$ Variance |
| 5 | **Rayleigh** | $\frac{2(z-a)}{b^2} e^{-(z-a)^2/b^2}$ | $a + b\sqrt{\pi}/2$ | $\frac{b^2(4-\pi)}{4}$ | Used for skewed |
| 6 | **Exponential** | $ae^{-az}, z \ge 0$ | $1/a$ | $1/a^2$ | Special Rayleigh case |
| 7 | **Erlang (Gamma)** | $\frac{a^b z^{b-1}}{(b-1)!} e^{-az}$ | $b/a$ | $b/a^2$ | Order $b$ |
| 8 | **Speckle** | $g = f \cdot u, \bar u = 1$ | $\bar f$ | $\sigma_u^2 \bar f^2$ | Multiplicative |
| 9 | **Periodic** | $A\sin(2\pi u_0 x + \phi)$ | 0 | $A^2/2$ | Frequency $u_0$ |
| 10 | **SNR** | $\mu_s / \sigma_n$ | — | — | Quality metric |
| 11 | **PSNR (dB)** | $10\log_{10}(L^2 / \text{MSE})$ | — | — | Reconstruction quality |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb:** Poisson noise in image $z$ of mean $\lambda$ is approximately Gaussian with $\sigma = \sqrt{\lambda}$ when $\lambda \ge 10$. This is why Gaussian filters often suffice for moderate-illumination X-ray images.

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Dose Optimization in CT:** Knowing that Poisson noise has variance $\lambda$ allows radiologists to halve dose only if $\lambda$ doubles, since SNR $\propto \sqrt{\text{dose}}$.
- **Ultrasound Speckle Reduction:** Because speckle is multiplicative, the **log transform** $z' = \log(z)$ converts it to additive noise, after which standard filters can be applied.
- **MRI Denoising:** Modern deep-learning networks explicitly learn the **Rician noise distribution** to avoid biasing magnitude images.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mean and Variance of Gaussian Noise (Verification)

We must prove that the Gaussian PDF yields mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$.

**Step 1 — Mean Definition:**

$$\bar{z} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} z \, p(z) \, dz = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} z \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-(z-\mu)^2/2\sigma^2} \, dz$$

**Step 2 — Substitution:** Let $t = (z - \mu)/\sigma$, so $z = \sigma t + \mu$ and $dz = \sigma \, dt$. The exponential becomes $e^{-t^2/2}$.

$$\bar{z} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (\sigma t + \mu) e^{-t^2/2} \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Split the integral:**

$$\bar{z} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} t \, e^{-t^2/2} \, dt + \frac{\mu}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-t^2/2} \, dt$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate both integrals.** The first integrand is **odd**, so it vanishes. The second equals $\sqrt{2\pi}$.

$$\bar{z} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \cdot 0 + \frac{\mu}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{2\pi} = \mu$$

**Step 5 — Variance Definition:**

$$\sigma_z^2 = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (z - \mu)^2 \, p(z) \, dz$$

**Step 6 — Substitute again** $t = (z-\mu)/\sigma$:

$$\sigma_z^2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (\sigma t)^2 \, e^{-t^2/2} \, dt = \frac{\sigma^2}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} t^2 e^{-t^2/2} \, dt$$

**Step 7 — Standard Gaussian moment** $\int t^2 e^{-t^2/2} dt = \sqrt{2\pi}$ (this is the second moment of standard normal).

$$\sigma_z^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{2\pi} = \sigma^2$$

$\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: SNR Relationship with Dose in X-ray Imaging

X-ray quantum noise arises from the random detection of photons. Let $N$ = mean number of photons per detector element (proportional to dose $D$).

**Step 1:** The number of detected photons follows Poisson: $P(k) = N^k e^{-N}/k!$, so $\mu_k = N$ and $\sigma_k^2 = N$.

**Step 2 — Definition of SNR:**

$$\text{SNR} = \frac{\mu_k}{\sigma_k} = \frac{N}{\sqrt{N}} = \sqrt{N}$$

**Step 3 — Dose proportionality:** $N = \alpha D$ for some constant $\alpha$ (system gain).

$$\text{SNR} = \sqrt{\alpha D}$$

**Step 4 — To double the SNR, we must quadruple the dose.** This is the fundamental reason why low-dose CT is so noisy and why dose reduction is non-linear in quality loss.

$$\text{SNR}_2 / \text{SNR}_1 = \sqrt{D_2 / D_1}$$

$\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Speckle Noise → Additive via Log Transform

Given $g = f \cdot u$ with $\bar{u} = 1$ and $u$ statistically independent of $f$.

**Step 1 — Take the logarithm:**

$$\log g = \log f + \log u$$

**Step 2 — Let** $g' = \log g$, $f' = \log f$, $u' = \log u$. The new noise $u'$ is **additive**:

$$g' = f' + u'$$

**Step 3 — Mean of transformed noise** (using Taylor expansion of $\log u$ around $\bar{u} = 1$):

$$\bar{u'} = E[\log u] \approx E[u - 1] = 0$$

**Step 4 — Variance:**

$$\sigma_{u'}^2 = E[(\log u)^2] \approx \sigma_u^2$$

Hence speckle can be suppressed by applying a standard mean or median filter in the log domain, then exponentiating back.

$\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Estimating Noise Parameters from an Image

| Goal | Formula | Steps |
|---|---|---|
| Estimate Gaussian $\sigma$ | $\hat{\sigma} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{MN-1} \sum_{x,y} (g(x,y) - \bar{g})^2}$ | Sample flat patch of size $M \times N$ |
| Estimate Poisson $\lambda$ | $\hat{\lambda} = \bar{g}$ in flat region | Mean = variance in flat patch |
| Estimate impulse noise prob. | $\hat{P} = (\text{#salt/pepper pixels})/(\text{total pixels})$ | Threshold extreme values |
| Compute image SNR | $\text{SNR} = \mu_{\text{ROI}} / \sigma_{\text{background}}$ | Pick signal & background ROIs |
| Compute PSNR (vs ground truth) | $10\log_{10}(L^2 / \text{MSE})$ dB, $L$ = peak value (255) | Compare with reference image |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** When asked to "comment on the noise characteristics," always specify:
> 1. The **noise type** (visual appearance + histogram shape),
> 2. The **PDF** governing it,
> 3. The **mean and variance** (or signal-dependence),
> 4. The **modality of origin** (e.g., "Poisson due to photon-counting statistics in low-dose CT").

---
