# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]
**Define shape descriptors in medical image processing. List any four commonly used shape descriptors.**

**Model Answer**:
Shape descriptors are quantitative geometric measurements extracted from the boundary and interior of a segmented region in a medical image, used to characterize the object's morphology.

Four commonly used shape descriptors are:
1. **Area** — total number of pixels inside the region.
2. **Perimeter** — total length of the region's boundary.
3. **Compactness (Circularity)** — $C = \frac{4\pi A}{P^2}$, ranging from 0 to 1.
4. **Eccentricity** — ratio of major to minor axis of the best-fit ellipse.

*Examiner's note: Students often forget to define the *range* of compactness; stating "0 to 1" earns a full mark.*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]
**What is a Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM)? Write the formula for GLCM-based contrast.**

**Model Answer**:
A **GLCM** $P(i,j)$ is a square matrix that tabulates the frequency with which two gray levels $i$ and $j$ occur at a specified spatial offset $(d, \theta)$ in an image. The matrix is symmetric for $\theta = 0°$ and $180°$ combined.

**Contrast** quantifies local intensity variation:

$$\text{Contrast} = \sum_{i}\sum_{j} (i-j)^2 \, P(i,j)$$

High contrast indicates a heterogeneous region; low contrast indicates homogeneity.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]

**Q.A (a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the chain-code representation of a boundary. Discuss the **first-difference chain code** and its rotation-invariant normalization.

**Q.A (b) [7 Marks]** Derive the expressions for the **central moments** of a 2D region. Using these, show how the **orientation of the major axis** is computed.

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a)

A **chain code** represents a digital boundary as a sequence of connected unit-length line segments in one of 8 directions (for 8-connectivity) or 4 directions (for 4-connectivity). Starting from an arbitrary pixel on the boundary, the direction of travel to the next boundary pixel is encoded as an integer $c_k \in \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7\}$.

The first-difference chain code is computed as:

$$d_k = (c_{k+1} - c_k) \mod 8, \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$$

This makes the representation **invariant to the choice of starting pixel** because shifting the starting point by 1 cyclically shifts all $d_k$ values by the same amount.

For **rotation invariance**, the first-difference sequence is treated as circular. The normalization is:

1. Compute all $N$ circular first-differences.
2. The rotation-normalized code is the one with the **smallest integer** when read cyclically.

This works because rotating the boundary by $45° \times r$ simply adds $r$ to every $c_k$, and hence to every $d_k$ (mod 8), preserving the relative pattern.

*Diagram: An 8-direction compass rose labeled N=0, NE=1, E=2, ... NW=7, with an example boundary traced through pixels in directions [0, 1, 2, 2, 3, 4, 4, 5].*

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b)

The **central moment** of order $(p+q)$ for a region $R$ with centroid $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{p,q} &= \sum_{(x,y)\in R} (x - \bar{x})^p (y - \bar{y})^q \\
\bar{x} &= \frac{\sum x}{N}, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{\sum y}{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derivation of orientation**:
The image's second-order central moments form the symmetric **inertia tensor** (covariance matrix of pixel coordinates):

$$
\mathbf{C} = \begin{pmatrix} \mu_{2,0} & \mu_{1,1} \\ \mu_{1,1} & \mu_{0,2} \end{pmatrix}
$$

The orientation $\theta$ of the major axis is the angle that diagonalizes $\mathbf{C}$. Solving the eigenvalue equation yields:

$$
\tan(2\theta) = \frac{2\mu_{1,1}}{\mu_{2,0} - \mu_{0,2}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\theta = \frac{1}{2}\arctan\!\left(\frac{2\mu_{1,1}}{\mu_{2,0} - \mu_{0,2}}\right)
$$

The angle $\theta$ lies in $(-\pi/2, \pi/2]$ and gives the rotation of the major axis from the horizontal.

*Examiner's note: The factor of 2 inside the arctan and the factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ outside are both required; forgetting either is a common 2-mark deduction.*

### Question B — Option 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]

**Q.B (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Fourier descriptor** method for shape representation. Derive the normalization steps to achieve **rotation, scale, and translation invariance**.

**Q.B (b) [7 Marks]** Define a **Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM)**. Compute the GLCM and the **contrast** and **homogeneity** features for the following 4×4 ROI quantized to 4 gray levels $\{0, 1, 2, 3\}$:
$$
I = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 2 & 2 & 3 & 3 \\ 2 & 2 & 3 & 3 \end{pmatrix}
$$
Use offset $d = 1$, $\theta = 0°$ (horizontal neighbors).

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a)

A Fourier descriptor represents a closed contour as a 1D discrete signal in the complex plane:

$$z(k) = x_k + j y_k, \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$$

The 1D DFT gives coefficients $a_n$:

$$a_n = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} z(k) e^{-j 2\pi n k / N}$$

**Invariance derivations**:

(i) **Translation**: A shift $\Delta z$ adds $\Delta z$ to every $z(k)$. By linearity of DFT, this adds $\Delta z$ to $a_0$ only. So we **discard $a_0$** to remove translation.

(ii) **Scale**: Multiplying $z(k)$ by constant $s$ multiplies every $a_n$ by $s$. So we **normalize by $\vert a_1 \vert$**:

$$\text{FD}_n = \frac{\vert a_n \vert}{\vert a_1 \vert}$$

(iii) **Rotation**: Rotation by angle $\phi$ multiplies $z(k)$ by $e^{j\phi}$, multiplying $a_n$ by $e^{j\phi}$. The **magnitude** $\vert a_n \vert$ is rotation-invariant, so we drop the phase.

The final invariant descriptor is:

$$\mathbf{f}_{\text{FD}} = \left[ \frac{\vert a_2 \vert}{\vert a_1 \vert}, \frac{\vert a_3 \vert}{\vert a_1 \vert}, \ldots, \frac{\vert a_{N-1} \vert}{\vert a_1 \vert} \right]$$

Only a few (typically 10–15) low-frequency coefficients are needed in practice, since high-frequency components encode noise.

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b)

A **GLCM** is a tabulation of how often pairs of gray levels $(i, j)$ occur at a specified spatial offset $(d, \theta)$ in an image. The matrix is square, of size $L \times L$ where $L$ is the number of gray levels.

**Computation for the given ROI, offset $d=1, \theta=0°$**:

For each pixel pair (left pixel $i$, right neighbor $j$):
- Row 0: $(0,0), (0,0), (1,1), (1,1)$ → $P(0,0) \mathrel{+}=2$, $P(1,1) \mathrel{+}=2$
- Row 1: $(0,0), (0,0), (1,1), (1,1)$ → $P(0,0) \mathrel{+}=2$, $P(1,1) \mathrel{+}=2$
- Row 2: $(2,2), (2,2), (3,3), (3,3)$ → $P(2,2) \mathrel{+}=2$, $P(3,3) \mathrel{+}=2$
- Row 3: $(2,2), (2,2), (3,3), (3,3)$ → $P(2,2) \mathrel{+}=2$, $P(3,3) \mathrel{+}=2$

Total pixel pairs: $N_p = 16$. Counts: $P(0,0) = 4$, $P(1,1) = 4$, $P(2,2) = 4$, $P(3,3) = 4$.

Normalized GLCM (each entry divided by 16):

$$
P = \begin{pmatrix} 0.25 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.25 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.25 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0.25 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Contrast**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Contrast} &= \sum_{i,j} (i-j)^2 P(i,j) \\
&= (0-0)^2(0.25) + (1-1)^2(0.25) + (2-2)^2(0.25) + (3-3)^2(0.25) \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Homogeneity**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Homogeneity} &= \sum_{i,j} \frac{P(i,j)}{1 + \vert i-j \vert} \\
&= \frac{0.25}{1+0} + \frac{0.25}{1+0} + \frac{0.25}{1+0} + \frac{0.25}{1+0} \\
&= 0.25 \times 4 = 1.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation**: The ROI is perfectly homogeneous — every horizontal neighbor pair has identical gray levels. Hence contrast = 0 and homogeneity = 1. This is the expected signature of a smooth, blocky intensity pattern.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions**:
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize the GLCM** by total pair count $N_p$ before computing features → 1–2 marks lost.
> 2. **Mixing up the contrast and dissimilarity formulas** → dissimilarity uses $\vert i - j \vert$, contrast uses $(i-j)^2$. Confusing them costs 2 marks.
> 3. **In Fourier descriptors**, forgetting the $\frac{1}{2}$ factor in orientation $\theta = \frac{1}{2}\arctan(\cdot)$ is the single most common 1-mark error in KTU valuation.
> 4. **Chain-code problems**: students often compute the **difference chain code** but skip the rotation-normalization step. Examiners explicitly check for circular-minimum normalization — losing 2–3 marks otherwise.
> 5. **Wrong Euler number formula**: writing $E_u = H - C$ instead of $E_u = C - H$ is a frequent slip; clarify on the answer sheet.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Shape descriptors** answer *"what does the object look like?"* — area, perimeter, compactness, eccentricity, moments, Fourier descriptors.
- **Content/texture descriptors** answer *"what is the object made of?"* — mean, variance, GLCM features, LBP, run-length, Gabor.
- **Compactness** $C = \frac{4\pi A}{P^2}$ equals 1 for a perfect disk, less for any other shape (isoperimetric inequality).
- **Centroid** is the normalized first-order moment $(\bar{x}, \bar{y}) = (M_{1,0}/M_{0,0},\; M_{0,1}/M_{0,0})$.
- **Central moments** are translation-invariant because the origin is shifted to the centroid.
- **Orientation** of major axis: $\theta = \frac{1}{2}\arctan\!\big(2\mu_{1,1}/(\mu_{2,0} - \mu_{0,2})\big)$.
- **Hu's 7 moments** are invariant to translation, rotation, and scale simultaneously — the gold standard for shape matching.
- **Fourier descriptors** sample the contour as $z_k = x_k + j y_k$ and apply 1D DFT; discarding $a_0$, normalizing by $\vert a_1 \vert$, and keeping magnitudes yields full affine invariance.
- **GLCM** is a $L \times L$ count matrix; entries $P(i,j)$ represent the joint probability of gray levels $(i,j)$ at offset $(d,\theta)$.
- **GLCM contrast** $\sum (i-j)^2 P(i,j)$ — zero for homogeneous images, high for speckled/heterogeneous images.
- **GLCM homogeneity** $\sum P(i,j)/(1+\vert i-j \vert)$ — unity for homogeneous, lower for textured.
- **Euler number** $E_u = C - H$ where $C$ = connected components, $H$ = holes; used in topology preservation checks.
- **Chain code** encodes boundary direction; first-difference is rotation-invariant up to a cyclic shift, normalized via the circular minimum.
- **Always** normalize a GLCM by total pair count before extracting features.
- **Clinical applications**: tumor grading (GLCM), lesion tracking (moments), mammography CAD (Fourier), organ volumetry (area).
- **Order of moment interpretation**: order 0 = mass, order 1 = centroid, order 2 = spread/orientation, order 3 = skewness, order 4 = kurtosis.
- **Invariance checklist** for any feature before deploying in clinical practice: translation? rotation? scale? illumination?
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification - Supervised and Unsupervised Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT604 - MEDICAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Classification - Supervised and Unsupervised Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Classification in Medical Image Processing?

In the context of **PBEBT604 – Medical Image Processing (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4)**, *classification* is the terminal stage of any Computer-Aided Detection/Diagnosis (CAD) pipeline. After image acquisition, preprocessing, segmentation, and feature extraction, the classification step assigns each region of interest (ROI), voxel, or pixel to one of a finite set of predefined clinical categories — for example, **"benign nodule"** vs. **"malignant tumour"**, or **"normal tissue"** vs. **"abnormal tissue"**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module-4 Definition (Exact Syllabus Terminology):**
> Classification is a pattern-recognition process that maps an input feature vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ to a discrete class label $\omega_j \in \{\omega_1, \omega_2, \ldots, \omega_c\}$ using a decision function learned from training samples.

The mapping is achieved by a **classifier** (also called a *hypothesis function* or *decision rule*):

$$h : \mathbb{R}^n \longrightarrow \{1, 2, \ldots, c\}$$

The two principal learning paradigms the KTU 2024 syllabus mandates are **Supervised Learning** and **Unsupervised Learning**.

---

## 1.2 Supervised Learning — The "Answer Key" Approach

### Formal Definition
**Supervised classification** uses a *labelled training set* $D = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$, where every feature vector $\mathbf{x}_i$ is paired with its known ground-truth class label $y_i \in \{\omega_1, \dots, \omega_c\}$. The algorithm learns an explicit input-to-output mapping $h(\mathbf{x}) \approx y$ that can then be applied to new, unlabelled test data.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Terminology — Three Phases of Supervised Learning:**
> 1. **Training phase:** Adjust classifier parameters using labelled samples.
> 2. **Validation phase:** Tune hyperparameters (e.g., $k$ in k-NN, $C$ in SVM).
> 3. **Testing phase:** Evaluate generalisation on held-out, unseen samples.

### Intuitive Analogy — A Radiologist Teaching a Resident
Imagine an experienced senior radiologist (the "supervisor") who shows a junior resident (the "student/classifier") 1,000 chest X-rays, *each one already diagnosed* — "this one is a malignant nodule, this one is benign, this one is normal." The resident studies the labelled examples, identifies visual/textural patterns, and builds an internal mental rule. Now when the resident is shown a *new, unlabelled* X-ray, they can classify it correctly. This is supervised learning.

### Common Supervised Classifiers in Medical Imaging (KTU 2024 Syllabus)
| Classifier | Core Principle | Typical Medical Use |
|---|---|---|
| Minimum Distance Classifier | Assign to nearest class mean in feature space | Quick lung-nodule screening |
| Bayes / Bayesian Classifier | Maximise posterior probability $P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x})$ | Risk-based diagnosis |
| k-Nearest Neighbour (k-NN) | Majority vote of $k$ closest training samples | Histology cell typing |
| Support Vector Machine (SVM) | Find maximum-margin separating hyperplane | Tumour / non-tumour classification |
| Decision Trees / Random Forest | Hierarchical if-then rules on features | Multi-class pathology grading |
| Artificial Neural Networks (ANN/CNN) | Non-linear layered feature transformations | Deep lesion detection |

---

## 1.3 Unsupervised Learning — Discovering Structure Without an Answer Key

### Formal Definition
**Unsupervised classification** (also called *clustering*) operates on an *unlabelled* dataset $D = \{\mathbf{x}_i\}_{i=1}^{N}$ with no ground-truth labels provided. The algorithm discovers intrinsic groupings by maximising intra-cluster similarity and inter-cluster dissimilarity, producing a partition $C = \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_k\}$ of the data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Distinction:**
> In **supervised** learning the number of classes $c$ is *known a priori* (provided by the labels). In **unsupervised** learning the number of clusters $k$ is a *design parameter* chosen by the user, not the data.

### Intuitive Analogy — Sorting Scans Into Trays
A lab technician receives 500 brain MRI slices *with no prior diagnosis attached*. The technician lays them on a table, then starts grouping them: "these 80 look similar in texture, these 60 look similar, these 70 are clearly different, …". Without any doctor telling them the categories, they discovered natural groupings based on feature similarity. This is unsupervised learning.

### Common Unsupervised Methods in Medical Imaging
| Method | Principle | Typical Medical Use |
|---|---|---|
| k-Means | Iterative centroid-based partitioning | Tissue segmentation (WM, GM, CSF) |
| Fuzzy c-Means (FCM) | Soft membership-based clustering | Brain-tissue segmentation with partial volumes |
| ISODATA | Self-organising k-means with split/merge | Initial lesion grouping |
| Self-Organising Map (SOM) | Topology-preserving neural clustering | Visualisation of high-D radiomic features |
| Hierarchical Clustering | Agglomerative / divisive dendrograms | Gene-expression / radiomic pattern discovery |
| PCA (as pre-processing) | Variance-preserving orthogonal projection | Dimensionality reduction before clustering |

---

## 1.4 Performance Metrics — Common Yardstick

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly tests these four metrics. All are derived from the **confusion matrix** built on a held-out test set.

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$

$$\text{Sensitivity (Recall / TPR)} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

$$\text{Specificity (TNR)} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}$$

$$\text{Precision (PPV)} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

$$\text{F1-score} = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$$

where **TP = True Positive**, **TN = True Negative**, **FP = False Positive**, **FN = False Negative**. In KTU valuation, students frequently lose marks by confusing *sensitivity* with *specificity* — remember: **sensitivity detects disease, specificity rules it out**.

---

## 1.5 Quick Reference Card

> [!TIP]
> **At a glance — Supervised vs Unsupervised**
> - **Data labels:** Supervised ✓ has labels | Unsupervised ✗ no labels
> - **Goal:** Supervised → learn mapping to known class | Unsupervised → discover hidden structure
> - **Output:** Supervised → class label | Unsupervised → cluster index
> - **Metric:** Supervised → accuracy, ROC-AUC | Unsupervised → silhouette, Davies-Bouldin index
> - **Medical example (supervised):** Detect lung cancer from CT features
> - **Medical example (unsupervised):** Segment white/grey matter in brain MRI
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Paradigms at a Glance

| Aspect | Supervised | Unsupervised |
|---|---|---|
| Training data $D$ | $\{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}$ labelled | $\{\mathbf{x}_i\}$ unlabelled |
| Number of classes $c$ | Known in advance | Unspecified; choose $k$ clusters |
| Learning objective | Minimise empirical risk $\sum L(h(\mathbf{x}_i), y_i)$ | Minimise within-cluster scatter $J = \sum_j \sum_{\mathbf{x}\in C_j} \Vert \mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j \Vert^2$ |
| Output | Decision function $h(\mathbf{x})$ | Cluster assignment $z_i \in \{1,\ldots,k\}$ |
| Evaluation | Confusion matrix, accuracy, ROC | Silhouette coefficient, intra/inter cluster ratio |
| KTU Module-4 example | Tumour vs normal cell (k-NN, SVM) | WM/GM/CSF segmentation (k-Means, FCM) |

---

## 2.2 The Bayes Classifier — The Theoretical Optimum

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Bayes classifier is the **asymptotic gold standard** the KTU examiner often tests. The Bayes rule is the *minimum-risk* classifier if the true distributions are known.

### Derivation of the Bayes Decision Rule
We want the classifier that **minimises the probability of error**. Given a feature vector $\mathbf{x}$, choose the class $\omega_j$ that maximises the posterior probability:

$$P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_j) \, P(\omega_j)}{p(\mathbf{x})}$$

Since $p(\mathbf{x})$ is common to all classes, the **Bayes decision rule** simplifies to:

$$\text{Decide } \omega^* = \arg\max_{j} \; p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_j) \, P(\omega_j)$$

For a *zero-one (symmetric) loss function* this rule is provably optimal and yields the **minimum probability of error**:

$$P_{\text{error}} = 1 - \int \max_j \, P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x}) \, p(\mathbf{x}) \, d\mathbf{x}$$

---

## 2.3 The Minimum Distance Classifier — Geometric Intuition

A frequently-tested simplification in the KTU 2024 paper is the **minimum (mean) distance classifier**. Assume each class $\omega_j$ is represented by a *prototypical mean vector* $\boldsymbol{\mu}_j$ (often estimated from training data). The classifier assigns a test sample to the nearest mean:

$$h(\mathbf{x}) = \arg\min_{j} \; d(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\mu}_j) \quad \text{where} \quad d(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\mu}_j) = \Vert \mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j \Vert^2$$

This is the Bayes-optimal classifier **only when**:
1. All classes are *equally likely* (uniform prior: $P(\omega_j) = 1/c$).
2. All classes share an *identical, isotropic, diagonal* covariance $\Sigma = \sigma^2 I$.

When these assumptions break down, we use the **Mahalanobis distance**:

$$d_M(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\mu}_j) = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j)^\top \Sigma_j^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j)}$$

---

## 2.4 k-Nearest Neighbour (k-NN) — A Lazy Learner

The k-NN classifier is *non-parametric*: it stores the entire training set and at query time looks at the $k$ closest training samples in feature space, then performs a **majority vote**.

**Algorithm (Valuation-Favourite Format):**
1. For query $\mathbf{x}_q$, compute distance to *every* training sample: $d_i = \Vert \mathbf{x}_q - \mathbf{x}_i \Vert$ (usually Euclidean, sometimes Mahalanobis).
2. Sort distances ascending; retain the $k$ smallest.
3. Assign $\mathbf{x}_q$ to the class with the highest vote count.

The decision rule is:

$$h(\mathbf{x}_q) = \arg\max_{j} \sum_{i \in N_k(\mathbf{x}_q)} \mathbb{1}(y_i = \omega_j)$$

where $N_k(\mathbf{x}_q)$ is the set of the $k$ nearest neighbours and $\mathbb{1}(\cdot)$ is the indicator function.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Trick:** For *k=1*, the decision boundary is the **Voronoi tessellation** of the training set. For odd $k$ in a binary problem, ties are avoided.

---

## 2.5 Support Vector Machine (SVM) — The Maximum-Margin Classifier

The SVM finds the hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ that **maximises the geometric margin** $2/\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$ between the two classes. The optimisation problem is:

$$\min_{\mathbf{w},b} \; \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i (\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1, \; \forall i$$

For *non-separable* data, slack variables $\xi_i \geq 0$ and a penalty $C$ are introduced (the **soft-margin SVM**):

$$\min_{\mathbf{w},b,\boldsymbol{\xi}} \; \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i \quad \text{s.t.} \quad y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$$

Kernel functions $\mathcal{K}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$ allow non-linear boundaries. The KTU syllabus covers:
- Linear kernel: $\mathcal{K}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j$
- Polynomial kernel: $\mathcal{K}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = (\gamma \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j + r)^d$
- Radial Basis Function (RBF): $\mathcal{K}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \Vert^2)$

---

## 2.6 k-Means Clustering — The Workhorse of Unsupervised Learning

The k-Means algorithm partitions $N$ samples into $k$ clusters by iteratively minimising the **within-cluster sum of squares (WCSS)**:

$$J(\mathcal{C}) = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_j} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j \Vert^2$$

**Lloyd's algorithm (KTU 2024 typical exam format):**
1. **Initialise:** Choose $k$ initial centroids $\{\boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(0)}, \ldots, \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{(0)}\}$ (randomly, or via k-means++).
2. **Assignment step:** Assign each sample to the nearest centroid — $z_i^{(t)} = \arg\min_j \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j^{(t-1)} \Vert^2$.
3. **Update step:** Recompute each centroid as the mean of its assigned samples — $\boldsymbol{\mu}_j^{(t)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_j \vert} \sum_{i: z_i^{(t)}=j} \mathbf{x}_i$.
4. Repeat 2–3 until convergence (centroids stop moving, or $J$ plateaus).

Convergence is guaranteed because $J$ monotonically decreases, but the result is sensitive to initialisation.

---

## 2.7 Fuzzy c-Means (FCM) — Soft Clustering

In medical imaging, voxels often contain *partial volumes* (e.g., a voxel that is 60% grey matter, 40% white matter). FCM allows each sample to belong to *all* clusters with a *membership grade* $u_{ij} \in [0,1]$ such that $\sum_{j=1}^{c} u_{ij} = 1$.

Minimise:

$$J_{FCM} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{c} u_{ij}^{m} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j \Vert^2 \quad m \in [1, \infty)$$

Iterative update equations:

$$u_{ij} = \frac{1}{\sum_{l=1}^{c} \left( \frac{\Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j \Vert}{\Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_l \Vert} \right)^{\frac{2}{m-1}}}$$

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_j = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} u_{ij}^{m} \, \mathbf{x}_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} u_{ij}^{m}}$$

The fuzzifier $m = 2$ is the de-facto standard in medical imaging literature.

---

## 2.8 Decision Tree — Hierarchical Rule Induction

A decision tree recursively partitions the feature space using axis-aligned splits that maximise the **information gain** (equivalently, minimise impurity):

$$\text{Gain}(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$$

where $H(S)$ is the Shannon entropy:

$$H(S) = -\sum_{j=1}^{c} p_j \log_2 p_j$$

The **Gini impurity** (used by CART) is faster to compute:

$$G(S) = 1 - \sum_{j=1}^{c} p_j^2$$

---

## 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table. Every line is a potential KTU 2024 ESE question.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Variables / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bayes Posterior | $P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_j) P(\omega_j)}{p(\mathbf{x})}$ | $p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_j)$: class-conditional PDF |
| 2 | Bayes Decision Rule | $\omega^* = \arg\max_j \, p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_j) P(\omega_j)$ | Minimum-error classification |
| 3 | Bayes Risk (general) | $R = \sum_i \sum_j L_{ij} \, P(\omega_i \mid \mathbf{x}) \, p(\mathbf{x}) \, d\mathbf{x}$ | $L_{ij}$: loss for deciding $\omega_j$ when truth is $\omega_i$ |
| 4 | Minimum Distance | $h(\mathbf{x}) = \arg\min_j \Vert \mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j \Vert^2$ | Euclidean baseline |
| 5 | Mahalanobis Distance | $d_M = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^\top \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})}$ | $\Sigma$: class covariance |
| 6 | k-NN Vote | $h(\mathbf{x}) = \arg\max_j \sum_{i \in N_k} \mathbb{1}(y_i = \omega_j)$ | $N_k$: $k$ nearest neighbours |
| 7 | SVM Primal (hard) | $\min \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w}\Vert^2$ s.t. $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$ | Linearly separable |
| 8 | SVM Primal (soft) | $\min \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w}\Vert^2 + C\sum \xi_i$ | $C$: penalty; $\xi_i$: slack |
| 9 | Kernel RBF | $\mathcal{K}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \Vert^2)$ | $\gamma > 0$ controls width |
| 10 | k-Means Objective | $J = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_j} \Vert \mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j \Vert^2$ | WCSS — must be minimised |
| 11 | FCM Membership | $u_{ij} = 1 / \sum_l (\Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j \Vert / \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_l \Vert)^{2/(m-1)}$ | $m$: fuzzifier |
| 12 | FCM Centroid | $\boldsymbol{\mu}_j = \sum_i u_{ij}^m \mathbf{x}_i / \sum_i u_{ij}^m$ | Weighted by membership |
| 13 | Shannon Entropy | $H(S) = -\sum_j p_j \log_2 p_j$ | Decision-tree split criterion |
| 14 | Gini Impurity | $G(S) = 1 - \sum_j p_j^2$ | CART split criterion |
| 15 | Information Gain | $\text{Gain}(S,A) = H(S) - \sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | Feature $A$, value $v$ |
| 16 | Accuracy | $(TP+TN) / (TP+TN+FP+FN)$ | Overall correctness |
| 17 | Sensitivity / Recall / TPR | $TP / (TP+FN)$ | Catches positives (disease) |
| 18 | Specificity / TNR | $TN / (TN+FP)$ | Rules out negatives |
| 19 | Precision / PPV | $TP / (TP+FP)$ | Trustworthiness of positive call |
| 20 | F1-Score | $2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of P & R |
| 21 | ROC-AUC | $\int_0^1 \text{TPR}(\text{FPR}^{-1}(t)) \, dt$ | Threshold-independent metric |
| 22 | Silhouette Coefficient | $s(i) = \dfrac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$ | Unsupervised quality, $s \in [-1, 1]$ |
| 23 | Davies-Bouldin Index | $DB = \dfrac{1}{k} \sum_{j=1}^{k} \max_{l \neq j} \dfrac{S_j + S_l}{M_{jl}}$ | Lower is better; $S_j$: scatter, $M_{jl}$: separation |

---

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why the KTU 2024 panel tests this module:** Modern CAD systems (e.g., for breast cancer detection in mammograms, diabetic retinopathy in retinal fundus images, or Alzheimer's in MRI) are built directly on these classifiers. The choice between supervised and unsupervised has engineering consequences — supervised methods need *expert-annotated* training data (expensive, slow, sometimes unethical for rare diseases), while unsupervised methods scale to large unlabelled archives but offer weaker clinical guarantees.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 examiner typically allocates 7–8 marks of a 14-mark question to a *derivation*. We present the four derivations that are statistically most likely to appear in the ESE.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Bayes Decision Rule from Minimum-Error Probability

**Goal:** Show that the rule $\omega^* = \arg\max_j P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x})$ minimises the classification error.

**Step 1 — Write the error for a specific input.** If we choose class $\omega_j$ when the true posterior is $P(\omega_i \mid \mathbf{x})$, we make an error with probability $1 - P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x})$.

**Step 2 — Total probability of error.** Integrating over the entire input space:

$$P(\text{error}) = \int \left[ 1 - P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x}) \right] p(\mathbf{x}) \, d\mathbf{x} = 1 - \int P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x}) \, p(\mathbf{x}) \, d\mathbf{x}$$

**Step 3 — Minimise the error.** The integrand $P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x})$ is the only quantity we control through the choice of $j$. For each $\mathbf{x}$, the integral is minimised when $P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x})$ is **maximised**. Hence:

$$\omega^*(\mathbf{x}) = \arg\max_{j} \; P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x})$$

**Step 4 — Replace the posterior with Bayes' rule.** Since $p(\mathbf{x})$ is independent of $j$:

$$\omega^*(\mathbf{x}) = \arg\max_{j} \; \frac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_j) \, P(\omega_j)}{p(\mathbf{x})} = \arg\max_{j} \; p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_j) \, P(\omega_j)$$

This is the **Bayes minimum-error classifier**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Minimum Distance Classifier as a Bayes Classifier

**Goal:** Prove that under equal priors and identical isotropic covariances, the Bayes rule reduces to choosing the nearest class mean.

**Step 1 — Gaussian class-conditional density.**

$$p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_j) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{n/2} \vert \Sigma \vert^{1/2}} \exp\left( -\frac{1}{2} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j)^\top \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j) \right)$$

**Step 2 — Bayes discriminant.** The Bayes rule chooses the $j$ that maximises the log-discriminant:

$$g_j(\mathbf{x}) = \log p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_j) + \log P(\omega_j)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Gaussian.** Drop the constant term $(2\pi)^{-n/2}$ and $\log \vert \Sigma \vert^{1/2}$ (independent of $j$ when $\Sigma$ is shared):

$$g_j(\mathbf{x}) = -\frac{1}{2} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j)^\top \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j) + \log P(\omega_j)$$

**Step 4 — Apply the assumptions.** Equal priors: $\log P(\omega_j) = -\log c$ (constant). Isotropic covariance: $\Sigma = \sigma^2 I$, so $\Sigma^{-1} = \frac{1}{\sigma^2} I$:

$$g_j(\mathbf{x}) = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \Vert \mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j \Vert^2 + \text{const}$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion.** Maximising $g_j$ is equivalent to minimising $\Vert \mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j \Vert^2$:

$$\omega^* = \arg\min_j \; \Vert \mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j \Vert^2 \quad \blacksquare$$

This explains the name *minimum distance classifier* and the KTU-asked *limitations* — it fails badly when $\Sigma_j$ are not equal.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: k-Means Convergence and WCSS Monotonicity

**Goal:** Show that Lloyd's algorithm never increases the WCSS objective $J$.

**Step 1 — Assignment step reduces $J$.** Fix centroids $\{\boldsymbol{\mu}_j\}$. For sample $\mathbf{x}_i$, the assignment $z_i = \arg\min_j \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j \Vert^2$ gives the smallest possible contribution to $J$ *for the current centroids*. Therefore, $J^{(t+1)} \leq J^{(t)}_{\text{assignment}}$.

**Step 2 — Update step reduces $J$.** Fix assignments $\{z_i\}$. The new centroid $\boldsymbol{\mu}_j^{\text{new}}$ is the mean of points in $C_j$, which minimises the squared error to those points:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_j^{\text{new}} = \arg\min_{\boldsymbol{\mu}} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_j} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu} \Vert^2$$

Setting $\partial / \partial \boldsymbol{\mu} = 0$ gives $2 \sum (\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}) = 0$, hence $\boldsymbol{\mu} = \frac{1}{\vert C_j \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_j} \mathbf{x}_i$. Therefore, $J^{(t+1)} \leq J^{(t)}_{\text{update}}$.

**Step 3 — Combined inequality.** Each full iteration satisfies $J^{(t+1)} \leq J^{(t)}$. Since $J \geq 0$ is bounded below, the algorithm must converge in finite steps (or to a fixed point) — although convergence to a *global* minimum is **not** guaranteed. $\blacksquare$

> [!TIP]
> This is a classic 5-mark derivation. KTU board examiners want the *partial derivative step* explicitly written.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: The SVM Dual and the Role of Support Vectors

**Goal:** Show that the optimal hyperplane is defined only by *support vectors* (samples on the margin).

**Step 1 — Write the Lagrangian of the hard-margin SVM.**

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \left[ y_i (\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \right]$$

where $\alpha_i \geq 0$ are the Lagrange multipliers.

**Step 2 — Stationarity with respect to $\mathbf{w}$ and $b$.**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{w} - \sum_i \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \mathbf{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = -\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back to obtain the dual problem.**

$$\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \; \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j$$

subject to $\alpha_i \geq 0$ and $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$.

**Step 4 — KKT complementary slackness.**

$$\alpha_i \left[ y_i (\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \right] = 0$$

This implies $\alpha_i > 0 \iff y_i (\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) = 1$ (samples *exactly on the margin*). All other training samples have $\alpha_i = 0$ and **do not influence** $\mathbf{w}$ — hence the name *support vectors*. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiner tip: substitute $\mathcal{K}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ in place of $\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j$ in step 3 to obtain the **kernelised dual** — this is the basis of non-linear SVM.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (Sample KTU 2024 ESE Style)

**Question:** A 2-class medical classification problem has training feature vectors with class means $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 = (2, 1)^\top$ and $\boldsymbol{\mu}_2 = (5, 4)^\top$ and equal isotropic covariance $\sigma^2 = 2$. Class priors are $P(\omega_1) = 0.7$ and $P(\omega_2) = 0.3$. Classify the test sample $\mathbf{x}_q = (3, 2)^\top$ using the **Bayes discriminant function**.

**Solution:**

Discriminant function (shared covariance, normal density):

$$g_j(\mathbf{x}) = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \Vert \mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j \Vert^2 + \log P(\omega_j)$$

Compute squared distances:
- $d_1^2 = (3-2)^2 + (2-1)^2 = 1 + 1 = 2$
- $d_2^2 = (3-5)^2 + (2-4)^2 = 4 + 4 = 8$

Compute discriminants:
- $g_1 = -\frac{2}{2(2)} + \log(0.7) = -0.5 + (-0.357) = -0.857$
- $g_2 = -\frac{8}{2(2)} + \log(0.3) = -2.0 + (-1.204) = -3.204$

Since $g_1 > g_2$, the classifier assigns $\mathbf{x}_q$ to class $\omega_1$. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation pitfall:** Students often forget the $\log P(\omega_j)$ prior term. With equal priors the answer would be $\omega_1$ (closer to $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1$), but with the *given* unequal priors the result is even more strongly in favour of $\omega_1$ — the prior reinforces the geometric decision. Always include the log-prior.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
Differentiate between **supervised** and **unsupervised** classification. Give **one medical imaging example** for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Supervised | Unsupervised |
|---|---|---|
| Training data | Labelled $(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)$ | Unlabelled $\{\mathbf{x}_i\}$ |
| Output | Predicted class label | Cluster index |
| Number of groups | Known a priori | Decided by algorithm/user |
| Evaluation | Confusion matrix, accuracy | Silhouette, DB index |

**Medical example (supervised):** A SVM trained on radiomic features of lung CT nodules labelled as benign/malignant by pathologists, then used to predict malignancy of new nodules.

**Medical example (unsupervised):** k-Means applied to brain MRI voxel intensities to segment white matter, grey matter, and CSF without any prior anatomical labels.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall (–1 mark):** Students write "supervised uses training data, unsupervised does not." This is wrong — unsupervised *also* uses data; it simply lacks **labels**. Be precise: *unlabelled training data* is the correct phrase.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
List the four entries of a **confusion matrix** for a binary medical classifier and define **sensitivity** and **specificity** in terms of these entries.

**Model Answer:**

| | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | TP (True Positive) | FN (False Negative) |
| **Actual Negative** | FP (False Positive) | TN (True Negative) |

- **Sensitivity (Recall, TPR):** $\text{Sensitivity} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ — the fraction of *actual diseased* patients correctly identified.
- **Specificity (TNR):** $\text{Specificity} = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ — the fraction of *actual healthy* patients correctly identified.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall (–1 mark):** Swapping the definitions. Mnemonic — *"S**P**ecificity rules out, S**N**S catches disease."* Or: *Sensitivity → SnNout* (a positive test rules **out** disease only when sensitivity is high); *Specificity → SpPin* (a positive test **rules in** disease when specificity is high).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4, Set A]**

**(a)** Derive the **Bayes minimum-error decision rule** for a 2-class medical classification problem. Show all steps. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a block diagram, explain the **k-Nearest Neighbour (k-NN) classifier**. Discuss the effect of the choice of $k$ and distance metric on classification performance in medical imaging. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1 — Statement of the problem.** Given a feature vector $\mathbf{x}$ drawn from one of two classes $\{\omega_1, \omega_2\}$ with priors $P(\omega_1), P(\omega_2)$ and class-conditional PDFs $p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_1), p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_2)$, find the rule that **minimises the probability of misclassification**.

**Step 2 — Express the error.** If we decide $\omega_1$, the conditional error is $1 - P(\omega_1 \mid \mathbf{x})$; if we decide $\omega_2$, it is $1 - P(\omega_2 \mid \mathbf{x})$. To minimise, pick the class with the **larger posterior** for each $\mathbf{x}$:

$$\omega^*(\mathbf{x}) = \arg\max_j \; P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x})$$

**Step 3 — Bayes' theorem.** $P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_j) P(\omega_j)}{p(\mathbf{x})}$.

**Step 4 — Decision rule.** Since $p(\mathbf{x}) > 0$ is common to both classes, the rule simplifies to:

$$\text{Decide } \omega_1 \text{ if } p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_1) P(\omega_1) > p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_2) P(\omega_2); \text{ else } \omega_2$$

**Step 5 — Equivalently in log form:**

$$\text{Decide } \omega_1 \text{ if } \log p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_1) - \log p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_2) > \log \frac{P(\omega_2)}{P(\omega_1)}$$

**Step 6 — Minimum-error probability.** The resulting probability of error is:

$$P_e = 1 - \int \max_j \, P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x}) \, p(\mathbf{x}) \, d\mathbf{x}$$

This is the **Bayes error** — the lowest achievable error rate. $\blacksquare$

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Block diagram (use Mermaid from Section 4.1 as a starting point — describe the k-NN module in words):**

1. **Input:** Query feature vector $\mathbf{x}_q$ (extracted from new medical image).
2. **Distance computation:** For each of the $N$ training samples $\mathbf{x}_i$, compute $d_i = \Vert \mathbf{x}_q - \mathbf{x}_i \Vert$ (typically Euclidean: $d_i = \sqrt{\sum_{m=1}^{n} (x_{q,m} - x_{i,m})^2}$).
3. **Sort & select:** Sort $\{d_i\}$ ascending; keep the $k$ smallest — these are the $k$ nearest neighbours $N_k(\mathbf{x}_q)$.
4. **Vote:** Assign $\mathbf{x}_q$ to the class with the highest count:
$$h(\mathbf{x}_q) = \arg\max_{j \in \{1,2\}} \sum_{i \in N_k(\mathbf{x}_q)} \mathbb{1}(y_i = \omega_j)$$
5. **Output:** Class label of $\mathbf{x}_q$.

**Effect of $k$:**
- $k = 1$ → highly flexible decision boundary (Voronoi cells), prone to noise and overfitting.
- $k$ large → smoother boundary, but may underfit and ignore local class structure.
- Optimal $k$ is found by cross-validation on a labelled validation set; an *odd* $k$ avoids ties in binary problems.

**Effect of distance metric:**
- **Euclidean** — assumes all features are equally scaled; sensitive to outliers. Works for compact, spherical clusters.
- **Mahalanobis** — accounts for feature covariance; preferred when features are correlated (e.g., radiomic texture features).
- **Cosine similarity** — used when magnitude is not meaningful (e.g., histogram-based features).
- **City-block (Manhattan)** — faster; robust to outliers.

**Medical imaging implications:** In tumour classification, $k = 5$ to $k = 15$ with Euclidean or Mahalanobis distance is common. Feature scaling (e.g., z-score normalisation) is *essential* — k-NN is distance-sensitive.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4, Set B]**

**(a)** Explain the **k-Means clustering algorithm** with its objective function and convergence criterion. How would you apply it to segment a brain MRI into white matter, grey matter, and CSF? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare the **decision boundary geometry** of the Minimum Distance Classifier, the Bayes Classifier with full Gaussian assumptions, and the k-NN classifier. Draw representative diagrams and explain when each is preferable in a medical-imaging context. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Objective function.** k-Means partitions $N$ feature vectors into $k$ disjoint clusters $\{C_1, \ldots, C_k\}$ by minimising the **within-cluster sum of squares (WCSS)**:

$$J = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_j} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j \Vert^2, \quad \boldsymbol{\mu}_j = \frac{1}{\vert C_j \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_j} \mathbf{x}_i$$

**Lloyd's algorithm:**

1. **Initialisation:** Choose $k = 3$ initial centroids $\{\boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(0)}, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2^{(0)}, \boldsymbol{\mu}_3^{(0)}\}$ — e.g., by k-means++ or by sampling three voxels of expected tissue intensity (low for CSF, medium for GM, high for WM on T1-weighted MRI).
2. **Assignment step (iterate $t = 1, 2, \ldots$):** For every voxel $\mathbf{x}_i$,
$$z_i^{(t)} = \arg\min_{j \in \{1,2,3\}} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j^{(t-1)} \Vert^2$$
3. **Update step:** Recompute each centroid as the mean of its assigned voxels.
4. **Convergence check:** Stop when centroids no longer change (i.e., $\Vert \boldsymbol{\mu}_j^{(t)} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j^{(t-1)} \Vert < \epsilon$) or when $J$ plateaus.

**Convergence guarantee:** $J$ is non-increasing and bounded below by 0, so the algorithm converges in finite steps (though possibly to a local minimum).

**Application to brain MRI segmentation:**
- **Input:** A T1-weighted MRI volume (voxel intensities in Hounsfield-like units).
- **Preprocess:** Apply intensity normalisation (e.g., Nyúl–Udupa histogram matching) and skull-stripping.
- **Feature vector per voxel:** $\mathbf{x}_i = (I_i)$ (intensity) or a richer vector $(I_i, \nabla I_i, \text{local mean})$.
- **Run k-Means with $k=3$:** Cluster 1 = CSF, Cluster 2 = Grey Matter, Cluster 3 = White Matter (after mapping the cluster centroids to tissue labels using anatomical prior — e.g., the cluster with the *lowest* centroid intensity is CSF).
- **Postprocess:** Apply connected-component analysis and morphological smoothing to remove spurious small clusters.
- **Output:** A labelled mask of WM, GM, CSF.

> [!TIP]
> Real clinical systems (FSL FAST, SPM) use **Fuzzy c-Means** or **Gaussian Mixture Models with spatial priors** for better robustness; k-Means is a teaching baseline.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Comparative analysis:**

| Aspect | Min. Distance Classifier | Bayes (full Gaussian) | k-NN |
|---|---|---|---|
| Boundary shape | Straight line (perpendicular bisector of means) | General quadratic (ellipse, parabola, hyperbola) | Polygonal / staircase (Voronoi cells) |
| Assumes | Equal priors, identical isotropic covariance | Gaussian class-conditional, possibly distinct covariances | No parametric assumption |
| Parametric? | Yes (just $\boldsymbol{\mu}_j$) | Yes ($\boldsymbol{\mu}_j, \Sigma_j, P(\omega_j)$) | No (instance-based) |
| Training cost | O(1) — store means | O(1) — store stats | O(N) — store all data |
| Test cost | O(n) per sample | O(n) per sample | O(Nn) per sample |
| Sensitivity to noise | Low (averages reduce noise) | Low–medium | High (especially for small $k$) |

**Diagrams:** (See Section 4.3 for the Mermaid block; in your answer sketch by hand — straight line for MDC, ellipse for Bayes, polygons for k-NN.)

**When to use in medical imaging:**
- **MDC:** When features are roughly equally scaled, classes are well-separated, and you need a *fast, interpretable* baseline. Common in early-stage CAD screening where interpretability matters.
- **Bayes (full Gaussian):** When you have enough training samples to estimate full covariance matrices reliably (e.g., $N \geq 10n$). Useful for risk-based diagnosis where asymmetric misclassification costs matter.
- **k-NN:** When the underlying class distributions are complex or non-Gaussian (e.g., histology nuclei), and you have a moderate dataset. Compute cost can be a bottleneck — use KD-trees or FAISS for acceleration.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways KTU students lose marks on this topic (from past board-exam analysis):**
> 1. **Confusing the Bayes rule with the minimum-distance rule.** They are *equivalent* **only** under equal priors and identical isotropic covariances — the derivation is in Section 3.2. Always state the assumptions before invoking either.
> 2. **Mixing up sensitivity and specificity.** Mnemonic: **SnNout, SpPin** (see Q2 above).
> 3. **Forgetting the prior term $\log P(\omega_j)$ in Bayes discriminant** — this alone can flip a classification result.
> 4. **Confusing the assignment step and update step of k-Means.** The assignment step is the *outer* loop; some students write the update first. Correct order: initialise → assign → update → check.
> 5. **Writing "k-Means converges to the global minimum."** It does **not** — only a *local* minimum. State this explicitly to gain a mark.
> 6. **Claiming SVM works for multi-class "out of the box."** Native SVM is binary; multi-class needs one-vs-rest or one-vs-one.
> 7. **Using the same test data for training and testing** — this gives a falsely high accuracy (overfitting). KTU expects a held-out test set or k-fold cross-validation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section. It is your 2-minute revision before walking into the KTU 2024 ESE hall.**

- ☐ Classification is the **final** stage of a medical image processing pipeline: *Image → Preprocess → Segment → Feature → Classify*.
- ☐ **Supervised** = labelled data, learn a mapping to known classes. **Unsupervised** = unlabelled data, discover groupings.
- ☐ **Bayes rule** $\omega^* = \arg\max_j p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_j) P(\omega_j)$ — the theoretical optimum; minimum-error.
- ☐ **Minimum distance classifier** is a *special case* of Bayes with equal priors and equal isotropic covariance.
- ☐ **k-NN**: lazy, non-parametric, uses majority vote of $k$ nearest training samples; sensitive to feature scaling.
- ☐ **SVM**: finds max-margin hyperplane; dual form depends only on *support vectors*; use RBF kernel for non-linear boundaries; parameter $C$ trades margin width against training error.
- ☐ **k-Means** alternates *assignment* and *update*; minimises WCSS; converges to a *local* minimum; sensitive to initialisation; use **k-means++** for better seeds.
- ☐ **Fuzzy c-Means** allows soft memberships $\sum_j u_{ij} = 1$; preferred over k-Means for **partial-volume** voxels in MRI.
- ☐ **Confusion matrix:** TP, FN, FP, TN — and from these: Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, Precision, F1.
- ☐ **ROC-AUC** = threshold-independent summary; AUC = 1.0 is perfect, 0.5 is random.
- ☐ **Unsupervised evaluation** uses internal indices (Silhouette, Davies-Bouldin) since no labels exist.
- ☐ **Real medical examples to remember for the exam:** k-Means/FCM for brain-tissue segmentation; SVM for lung-nodule and breast-cancer classification; CNNs (mentioned only) for lesion detection in X-rays.
- ☐ **Always normalise features** (z-score or min-max) before distance-based classifiers (k-NN, MDC, k-Means).
- ☐ **Always use cross-validation** for hyperparameter tuning; never report accuracy on the training set.
- ☐ **Notation to write cleanly:** $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ feature vector, $y \in \{1, \ldots, c\}$ class label, $\boldsymbol{\mu}_j$ class mean, $\Sigma_j$ class covariance, $P(\omega_j)$ prior, $p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_j)$ likelihood.
- ☐ **Bayes error** = lower bound on classification error; achievable only if true distributions are known.
- ☐ **Decision tree** splits on information gain (entropy) or Gini impurity; random forest is an ensemble of decision trees.

> **END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — CLASSIFICATION (SUPERVISED & UNSUPERVISED LEARNING)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discriminant Analysis (DA)** is a supervised statistical pattern-recognition technique used in medical image processing to find a linear combination of features (an *optimal projection axis*) that best separates two or more classes of medical data — for example, **malignant vs. benign tissue**, **tumour vs. normal parenchyma**, or **Alzheimer's vs. healthy brain** scans.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, the most relevant variant is **Fisher's Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA)**, which projects high-dimensional medical feature vectors (intensity, texture, shape, GLCM descriptors, wavelet coefficients) onto a low-dimensional subspace in a way that **maximises inter-class separability** while **minimising intra-class scatter**.

$$ \text{Goal of LDA:} \quad \max_{w} \;\; J(w) \;=\; \dfrac{w^{T} S_{B}\, w}{w^{T} S_{W}\, w} $$

where $S_{B}$ is the **between-class scatter matrix** and $S_{W}$ is the **within-class scatter matrix**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Sorting Hat"

Imagine a hospital pathology lab receiving thousands of cell images that must be sorted into two bins: **"Cancer"** and **"Normal"**. A junior technician looks at hundreds of features (nucleus size, chromatin density, edge sharpness, staining intensity). He needs a single straight line on a paper (a 1-D axis) such that:

- All cancer cells, when their features are plotted, fall on **one side** of the line.
- All normal cells fall on the **other side**.
- The two groups are **as far apart as possible**.
- Within each group, the points are **as tightly packed as possible**.

> That "best straight line" is exactly what **Linear Discriminant Analysis** computes. It is the mathematically optimal sorting axis, derived not by intuition but by **Fisher's criterion**.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Significance

| Aspect | Engineering Role |
|---|---|
| **Dimensionality Reduction** | Compresses 100s of medical features into 1–(c−1) discriminant axes |
| **Class Separability** | Provides a quantitative *figure of merit* for feature evaluation |
| **Pre-processing Step** | Feeds cleaner inputs to classifiers (k-NN, SVM, Bayesian) |
| **Visualisation** | Enables 2-D scatter plots of otherwise high-D medical features |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric in KTU Examinations:** *Fisher's Ratio* $J(w)$ is the canonical performance measure. The maximum value $J^{*}(w) = \lambda_{\max}$ equals the **largest generalised eigenvalue** of the pair $(S_{B}, S_{W})$.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Decomposition

The LDA pipeline can be decomposed into **seven logical stages**. Each stage has a precise mathematical purpose, summarised below.

### Stage 1 — Define the Classes
- Given a labelled medical dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x_{k}, y_{k}\}_{k=1}^{N}$ where $x_{k} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is a feature vector and $y_{k} \in \{1, 2, \dots, c\}$ is the class label.
- Partition the dataset into $c$ disjoint subsets $\mathcal{X}_{1}, \mathcal{X}_{2}, \dots, \mathcal{X}_{c}$ with cardinalities $N_{1}, N_{2}, \dots, N_{c}$ and $N = \sum_{i} N_{i}$.

### Stage 2 — Compute Class Means
- For each class $i$, compute the mean vector $\mu_{i}$:
$$ \mu_{i} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{N_{i}} \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}_{i}} x $$

### Stage 3 — Compute the Global (Overall) Mean
$$ \mu \;=\; \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} x_{k} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{c} N_{i}\, \mu_{i} $$

### Stage 4 — Construct the Within-Class Scatter Matrix $S_{W}$
This matrix quantifies the *spread of samples inside each class* (intra-class variance). A small $S_{W}$ means each class is compact — easier to separate.

$$ S_{W} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{c} \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}_{i}} (x - \mu_{i})\,(x - \mu_{i})^{T} $$

### Stage 5 — Construct the Between-Class Scatter Matrix $S_{B}$
This matrix quantifies the *separation between class means* relative to the global mean (inter-class variance). A large $S_{B}$ means the classes are well spread apart.

$$ S_{B} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{c} N_{i}\, (\mu_{i} - \mu)\,(\mu_{i} - \mu)^{T} $$

### Stage 6 — Solve the Generalised Eigenvalue Problem
The optimal projection direction $w$ is the eigenvector corresponding to the **largest generalised eigenvalue** of:

$$ S_{B}\, w \;=\; \lambda\, S_{W}\, w \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad S_{W}^{-1} S_{B}\, w \;=\; \lambda\, w $$

### Stage 7 — Multi-Class Extension
For $c$ classes, the **rank** of $S_{B}$ is at most $c-1$, so at most $c-1$ discriminant (projection) vectors can be extracted. These form a projection matrix $W = [w_{1}, w_{2}, \dots, w_{c-1}]$.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-critical cheat sheet** for any LDA problem in KTU ESE / Internal assessments. Bookmark it.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Dimensions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Class Mean | $\mu_{i} = \dfrac{1}{N_{i}} \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}_{i}} x$ | $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ |
| 2 | Global Mean | $\mu = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i} N_{i}\, \mu_{i}$ | $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ |
| 3 | Within-Class Scatter | $S_{W} = \sum_{i=1}^{c} \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}_{i}} (x - \mu_{i})(x - \mu_{i})^{T}$ | $d \times d$ symmetric PSD |
| 4 | Between-Class Scatter | $S_{B} = \sum_{i=1}^{c} N_{i} (\mu_{i} - \mu)(\mu_{i} - \mu)^{T}$ | $d \times d$ symmetric PSD |
| 5 | Total Scatter | $S_{T} = S_{W} + S_{B} = \sum_{k}(x_{k} - \mu)(x_{k} - \mu)^{T}$ | $d \times d$ |
| 6 | Fisher's Criterion | $J(w) = \dfrac{w^{T} S_{B} w}{w^{T} S_{W} w}$ | Scalar; to be **maximised** |
| 7 | Optimal Direction | $w^{*} \propto S_{W}^{-1} (\mu_{1} - \mu_{2})$ *(2-class case)* | $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ |
| 8 | Generalised Eigen-equation | $S_{B} w = \lambda S_{W} w$ | Roots give $\lambda$ |
| 9 | Max. Class. Value | $J^{*}(w^{*}) = \lambda_{\max}$ | Equals top eigenvalue |
| 10 | Max. Projections | $\text{rank}(S_{B}) \le c - 1$ | Use only top $(c-1)$ vectors |
| 11 | Projection of a sample | $y = W^{T} x$ | $y \in \mathbb{R}^{c-1}$ |
| 12 | Decision Rule (2-class) | Assign $x$ to $\omega_{1}$ if $w^{T} x > w_{0}$, else $\omega_{2}$ | Threshold $w_{0} = w^{T}(\frac{N_{1}\mu_{1} + N_{2}\mu_{2}}{N_{1}+N_{2}})$ |

## 2.3 Why Each Step Works — The "Why & How"

- **Why $S_{W}$?** When projecting onto $w$, the variance of class $i$ becomes $w^{T} S_{W_{i}} w$. Minimising the total of these variances makes each class a tight cluster.
- **Why $S_{B}$?** The squared distance between projected class means is $w^{T} S_{B} w$ (up to a constant). Maximising this pushes classes apart.
- **Why generalised eigenvalue?** Setting $\dfrac{dJ}{dw} = 0$ and using a Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$ produces exactly the equation $S_{B} w = \lambda S_{W} w$.
- **Why rank $\le c-1$?** Each $\mu_{i} - \mu$ lives in a $c-1$ dimensional affine subspace (the sum of all weighted deviations is zero), so the span of $S_{B}$ cannot exceed $c-1$.

## 2.4 Real-World Medical Engineering Utility

| Application | Role of LDA |
|---|---|
| **Brain MRI / Alzheimer's Detection** | Separates AD, MCI, and Control groups using hippocampal texture features |
| **Breast Tumour Classification** | Differentiates benign vs malignant masses on mammograms |
| **Lung Nodule Detection (CT)** | Reduces 3-D voxel features to a few discriminant axes feeding a CAD system |
| **Cell Nuclei Analysis** | Separates dysplastic from normal nuclei in Pap-smear cytology |
| **Retinal Vessel Analysis** | Identifies diabetic retinopathy severity grades from fundus images |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fisher's Criterion from First Principles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goal:** Show that maximising $J(w) = \dfrac{w^{T} S_{B} w}{w^{T} S_{W} w}$ leads to the generalised eigenvalue problem $S_{B} w = \lambda S_{W} w$.

**Setup.** Consider the two-class case. After projection onto a unit direction $w$, the projected mean of class $i$ is $\tilde{\mu}_{i} = w^{T} \mu_{i}$ (a scalar). The *between-class separation* (squared) is:

$$ (\tilde{\mu}_{1} - \tilde{\mu}_{2})^{2} \;=\; (w^{T} \mu_{1} - w^{T} \mu_{2})^{2} \;=\; w^{T} (\mu_{1} - \mu_{2})\,(\mu_{1} - \mu_{2})^{T} w $$

Because the two-class $S_{B} = N_{1}(\mu_{1} - \mu)(\mu_{1} - \mu)^{T} + N_{2}(\mu_{2} - \mu)(\mu_{2} - \mu)^{T}$ and one can show that for $c=2$, $S_{B} \propto (\mu_{1} - \mu_{2})(\mu_{1} - \mu_{2})^{T}$, the numerator of $J$ becomes the squared projected mean separation.

The within-class scatter (sum of projected variances) is:

$$ \tilde{S}_{W} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{2} \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}_{i}} (w^{T} x - w^{T} \mu_{i})^{2} \;=\; w^{T} S_{W} w $$

Hence Fisher's criterion is well-defined as:

$$ J(w) \;=\; \dfrac{w^{T} S_{B} w}{w^{T} S_{W} w} $$

**Step 1 — Differentiation.** Take the derivative of $J$ with respect to $w$ and set it to zero. Using the quotient rule for a scalar function of a vector:

$$ \dfrac{\partial J}{\partial w} \;=\; \dfrac{2 S_{B} w\,(w^{T} S_{W} w) - 2 S_{W} w\,(w^{T} S_{B} w)}{(w^{T} S_{W} w)^{2}} $$

**Step 2 — Setting to Zero.** Set the numerator to zero:

$$ S_{B} w\,(w^{T} S_{W} w) \;=\; S_{W} w\,(w^{T} S_{B} w) $$

**Step 3 — Recognise the Scalar Factors.** Notice that $(w^{T} S_{W} w)$ and $(w^{T} S_{B} w)$ are both scalars. Let:

$$ \lambda \;\equiv\; \dfrac{w^{T} S_{B} w}{w^{T} S_{W} w} \;\equiv\; J(w) $$

Then the equation becomes:

$$ S_{B} w \;=\; \lambda\, S_{W} w $$

This is the **generalised eigenvalue problem**. $\square$

**Step 4 — Two-Class Closed-Form Solution.** For $c = 2$, $S_{B} w = \lambda S_{W} w$ reduces to a one-dimensional problem. Since $S_{B}$ has rank 1 in the two-class case, the eigenvector is:

$$ w^{*} \;\propto\; S_{W}^{-1}(\mu_{1} - \mu_{2}) $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** $S_{W}^{-1}$ *whitens* the data (equalises within-class variances), and then we project along the direction of the *whitened mean difference*. This is the optimal discriminant direction.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Worked Numerical Example (2-D, 3-Class Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked example** — A 2-D medical feature space (e.g., "nucleus roundness" $x_{1}$ vs. "chromatin texture index" $x_{2}$), with **two classes** $\omega_{1}$ and $\omega_{2}$.

### Given Data

| Class | Samples |
|---|---|
| $\omega_{1}$ | $x_{1}^{(1)} = (1, 1)^{T},\;\; x_{2}^{(1)} = (2, 3)^{T},\;\; x_{3}^{(1)} = (3, 2)^{T}$ |
| $\omega_{2}$ | $x_{1}^{(2)} = (5, 4)^{T},\;\; x_{2}^{(2)} = (6, 6)^{T},\;\; x_{3}^{(2)} = (7, 5)^{T}$ |

### Step A — Compute Class Means

$$ \mu_{1} \;=\; \frac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 1+2+3 \\ 1+3+2 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} $$

$$ \mu_{2} \;=\; \frac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 5+6+7 \\ 4+6+5 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 6 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix} $$

### Step B — Compute Global Mean

$$ \mu \;=\; \frac{1}{6} \begin{bmatrix} 1+2+3+5+6+7 \\ 1+3+2+4+6+5 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 3.5 \end{bmatrix} $$

### Step C — Within-Class Scatter Matrix $S_{W}$

For $\omega_{1}$, compute deviations and outer products:

$$ x_{1}^{(1)} - \mu_{1} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \text{outer} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$

$$ x_{2}^{(1)} - \mu_{1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \text{outer} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$

$$ x_{3}^{(1)} - \mu_{1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \text{outer} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} $$

Sum for $\omega_{1}$: $\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$

For $\omega_{2}$, by symmetry, the sum is the same: $\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$

$$ S_{W} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix} $$

### Step D — Between-Class Scatter Matrix $S_{B}$

$$ \mu_{1} - \mu = \begin{bmatrix} -2 \\ -1.5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mu_{2} - \mu = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1.5 \end{bmatrix} $$

$$ (\mu_{1} - \mu)(\mu_{1} - \mu)^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 3 \\ 3 & 2.25 \end{bmatrix}, \quad (\mu_{2} - \mu)(\mu_{2} - \mu)^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 3 \\ 3 & 2.25 \end{bmatrix} $$

$$ S_{B} \;=\; 3 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 3 \\ 3 & 2.25 \end{bmatrix} + 3 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 3 \\ 3 & 2.25 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 24 & 18 \\ 18 & 13.5 \end{bmatrix} $$

### Step E — Compute $S_{W}^{-1}$

$$ \det(S_{W}) \;=\; 4 \cdot 4 - 2 \cdot 2 \;=\; 16 - 4 \;=\; 12 $$

$$ S_{W}^{-1} \;=\; \frac{1}{12} \begin{bmatrix} 4 & -2 \\ -2 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \tfrac{1}{3} & -\tfrac{1}{6} \\ -\tfrac{1}{6} & \tfrac{1}{3} \end{bmatrix} $$

### Step F — Form $S_{W}^{-1} S_{B}$

$$ S_{W}^{-1} S_{B} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \tfrac{1}{3} & -\tfrac{1}{6} \\ -\tfrac{1}{6} & \tfrac{1}{3} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 24 & 18 \\ 18 & 13.5 \end{bmatrix} $$

$$ =\; \begin{bmatrix} \tfrac{24}{3} - \tfrac{18}{6} & \tfrac{18}{3} - \tfrac{13.5}{6} \\ -\tfrac{24}{6} + \tfrac{18}{3} & -\tfrac{18}{6} + \tfrac{13.5}{3} \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 8 - 3 & 6 - 2.25 \\ -4 + 6 & -3 + 4.5 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 3.75 \\ 2 & 1.5 \end{bmatrix} $$

### Step G — Solve the Eigenvalue Problem $\det(S_{W}^{-1} S_{B} - \lambda I) = 0$

$$ \det \begin{bmatrix} 5 - \lambda & 3.75 \\ 2 & 1.5 - \lambda \end{bmatrix} \;=\; (5-\lambda)(1.5-\lambda) - 7.5 $$

$$ = 7.5 - 6.5\lambda + \lambda^{2} - 7.5 \;=\; \lambda^{2} - 6.5\lambda \;=\; \lambda(\lambda - 6.5) \;=\; 0 $$

So $\lambda_{1} = 0$ and $\lambda_{2} = 6.5$.

### Step H — Find the Dominant Eigenvector ($\lambda_{2} = 6.5$)

$$ \begin{bmatrix} 5 - 6.5 & 3.75 \\ 2 & 1.5 - 6.5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} w_{1} \\ w_{2} \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} $$

$$ -1.5\, w_{1} + 3.75\, w_{2} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; w_{1} = 2.5\, w_{2} $$

Choose $w_{2} = 1 \Rightarrow w_{1} = 2.5$. The (unnormalised) optimal direction is $w^{*} = (2.5, 1)^{T}$.

### Step I — Normalise and Project Class Means

$$ \Vert w^{*} \Vert \;=\; \sqrt{2.5^{2} + 1^{2}} \;=\; \sqrt{7.25} \;\approx\; 2.69 $$

$$ \hat{w}^{*} \;=\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{7.25}} \begin{bmatrix} 2.5 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \;\approx\; \begin{bmatrix} 0.928 \\ 0.371 \end{bmatrix} $$

Projected means:

$$ \hat{w}^{*T} \mu_{1} \;=\; 0.928(2) + 0.371(2) \;\approx\; 2.60 $$

$$ \hat{w}^{*T} \mu_{2} \;=\; 0.928(6) + 0.371(5) \;\approx\; 7.42 $$

The classes are well separated along $w^{*}$ (distance $\approx 4.82$). $\square$

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Boundary (2-Class)

The decision threshold for a 2-class LDA is the midpoint of the projected means:

$$ w_{0} \;=\; w^{T} \left( \frac{N_{1}\, \mu_{1} + N_{2}\, \mu_{2}}{N_{1} + N_{2}} \right) $$

A new sample $x$ is assigned to:
- $\omega_{1}$ if $w^{T} x > w_{0}$
- $\omega_{2}$ if $w^{T} x < w_{0}$

For our example ($N_{1} = N_{2} = 3$):

$$ w_{0} \;=\; \frac{1}{6} w^{T}(\mu_{1} + \mu_{2}) \;\approx\; \frac{2.60 + 7.42}{2} \;=\; 5.01 $$

## 3.4 Multi-Class Extension Summary

For $c$ classes, project onto the matrix $W = [w_{1}, w_{2}, \dots, w_{c-1}] \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times (c-1)}$ where each $w_{i}$ solves:

$$ S_{B} w_{i} \;=\; \lambda_{i}\, S_{W} w_{i} \quad \text{with} \quad \lambda_{1} \ge \lambda_{2} \ge \dots \ge \lambda_{c-1} $$

The transformed feature $y = W^{T} x \in \mathbb{R}^{c-1}$ is then fed to any downstream classifier (Bayes, k-NN, MLP, SVM).

    H --> I

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam – Model Question]`**

### Q1. Define within-class and between-class scatter matrices used in Linear Discriminant Analysis. Why are they important in medical image classification? **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **within-class scatter matrix** $S_{W}$ measures the spread of feature vectors *inside* each class. It is defined as:

$$ S_{W} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{c} \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}_{i}} (x - \mu_{i})\,(x - \mu_{i})^{T} $$

The **between-class scatter matrix** $S_{B}$ measures the separation of class *means* from the global mean:

$$ S_{B} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{c} N_{i}\, (\mu_{i} - \mu)\,(\mu_{i} - \mu)^{T} $$

**Importance in medical imaging:** A *small* $S_{W}$ indicates that pathological patterns (e.g., malignant cells) form a tight, consistent cluster. A *large* $S_{B}$ indicates that the mean feature signature of a disease class is far from the healthy baseline. Maximising the ratio $J(w) = w^{T} S_{B} w / w^{T} S_{W} w$ therefore optimises the diagnostic separation in computer-aided detection (CAD) systems.

---

### Q2. What is the rank of the between-class scatter matrix $S_{B}$ for a $c$-class problem, and what is its practical implication? **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The rank of $S_{B}$ is at most $c - 1$, i.e., $\text{rank}(S_{B}) \le c - 1$. This is because each deviation vector $(\mu_{i} - \mu)$ lies in a $c-1$ dimensional affine subspace, since the weighted sum $\sum_{i} N_{i}(\mu_{i} - \mu) = 0$.

**Practical implication:** Even if the original feature space is high-dimensional (e.g., $d = 100$), LDA can extract at most $c - 1$ discriminant directions. For a binary (tumour vs. normal) classification, only **one** projection axis is needed. This is a powerful *dimensionality reduction* property, often exploited in medical imaging to compress voxel-level features for downstream classification.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]`**

### Question A — Option 1: 14 Marks

#### (a) Derive the optimal projection direction in Fisher's Linear Discriminant Analysis for a two-class problem. Clearly state the role of $S_{W}$ and $S_{B}$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Problem Setup.** Let $\mathcal{X}_{1}$ and $\mathcal{X}_{2}$ be two classes of medical feature vectors, with means $\mu_{1}, \mu_{2} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ and within-class covariances aggregated into $S_{W}$. We seek a projection direction $w$ that maximises the separability of the projected means *while* minimising the projected within-class variance.

**Step 2 — Fisher's Criterion.** Define:

$$ J(w) \;=\; \frac{(\tilde{\mu}_{1} - \tilde{\mu}_{2})^{2}}{\tilde{S}_{1}^{2} + \tilde{S}_{2}^{2}} \;=\; \frac{w^{T} S_{B} w}{w^{T} S_{W} w} $$

where $S_{B} = (\mu_{1} - \mu_{2})(\mu_{1} - \mu_{2})^{T}$ for the 2-class case.

**Step 3 — Optimisation.** Differentiate $J(w)$ and set $\dfrac{dJ}{dw} = 0$:

$$ \frac{\partial J}{\partial w} = \frac{2 S_{B} w \cdot w^{T} S_{W} w - 2 S_{W} w \cdot w^{T} S_{B} w}{(w^{T} S_{W} w)^{2}} = 0 $$

Equivalently, multiplying by $(w^{T} S_{W} w)^{2}/2$:

$$ S_{B} w \cdot (w^{T} S_{W} w) \;=\; S_{W} w \cdot (w^{T} S_{B} w) $$

**Step 4 — Define the Lagrange-like Scalar.** Let $\lambda = \dfrac{w^{T} S_{B} w}{w^{T} S_{W} w}$. Then:

$$ S_{B} w \;=\; \lambda\, S_{W} w \quad\Longrightarrow\quad S_{W}^{-1} S_{B} w \;=\; \lambda\, w $$

**Step 5 — Closed-Form Solution.** Since $S_{B}$ has rank 1 in the two-class case, the dominant eigenvector is aligned with $(\mu_{1} - \mu_{2})$:

$$ w^{*} \;\propto\; S_{W}^{-1} (\mu_{1} - \mu_{2}) $$

This is the **Fisher's Linear Discriminant direction**. The maximum value of $J$ is $J(w^{*}) = \lambda_{\max}$, the largest eigenvalue.

**Role of $S_{W}$:** Normalises the within-class variance so that no single feature dominates purely due to scale.
**Role of $S_{B}$:** Captures the geometric separation of class means; maximising its projected form pushes the means apart.

---

#### (b) Consider the following 2-D medical feature dataset (nucleus roundness $x_{1}$, chromatin texture $x_{2}$) for two classes $\omega_{1}, \omega_{2}$:

| Class | Samples |
|---|---|
| $\omega_{1}$ | $(1, 1),\;(2, 3),\;(3, 2)$ |
| $\omega_{2}$ | $(5, 4),\;(6, 6),\;(7, 5)$ |

Compute $S_{W}$, $S_{B}$, and the optimal projection direction $w^{*}$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Class Means.**
$$ \mu_{1} = (2, 2)^{T}, \quad \mu_{2} = (6, 5)^{T} $$

**Step 2 — Within-Class Scatter.** For $\omega_{1}$:
$$ \sum (x-\mu_{1})(x-\mu_{1})^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} $$

For $\omega_{2}$ (same form):
$$ \sum (x-\mu_{2})(x-\mu_{2})^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} $$

$$ S_{W} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix} $$

**Step 3 — Global Mean.** $\mu = (4, 3.5)^{T}$.

**Step 4 — Between-Class Scatter.**
$$ \mu_{1} - \mu = (-2, -1.5)^{T},\quad \mu_{2} - \mu = (2, 1.5)^{T} $$

$$ S_{B} = 3 \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 3 \\ 3 & 2.25 \end{bmatrix} + 3 \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 3 \\ 3 & 2.25 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 24 & 18 \\ 18 & 13.5 \end{bmatrix} $$

**Step 5 — Inverse of $S_{W}$.** $\det(S_{W}) = 12$, hence:
$$ S_{W}^{-1} = \frac{1}{12}\begin{bmatrix} 4 & -2 \\ -2 & 4 \end{bmatrix} $$

**Step 6 — Two-Class Closed Form.**
$$ w^{*} \;\propto\; S_{W}^{-1}(\mu_{1} - \mu_{2}) \;=\; \frac{1}{12}\begin{bmatrix} 4 & -2 \\ -2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} -4 \\ -3 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \frac{1}{12}\begin{bmatrix} -16+6 \\ 8-12 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \frac{1}{12}\begin{bmatrix} -10 \\ -4 \end{bmatrix} \;\propto\; \begin{bmatrix} 2.5 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} $$

So the optimal discriminant direction is $w^{*} = (2.5, 1)^{T}$ (or its normalised form $\hat{w}^{*} \approx (0.928, 0.371)^{T}$). The maximum Fisher's ratio is $\lambda_{\max} = 6.5$.

---

### Question B — Option 2: 14 Marks (Internal Choice)

#### (a) Compare Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) and Principal Component Analysis (PCA). Discuss why LDA is generally preferred for *supervised* medical image classification. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | PCA | LDA |
|---|---|---|
| **Paradigm** | Unsupervised | Supervised (uses class labels) |
| **Objective** | Maximise total variance $w^{T} S_{T} w$ | Maximise $\dfrac{w^{T} S_{B} w}{w^{T} S_{W} w}$ |
| **What it finds** | Directions of largest *overall* spread | Directions of largest *class separability* |
| **Uses class labels** | No | Yes |
| **Max. components** | $\min(N-1, d)$ | $c - 1$ |
| **Best for** | Data compression, denoising | Classification, discriminant analysis |
| **Tumour/Normal example** | May mix tumour and normal points if they share variance axis | Pushes tumour and normal means apart |
| **Failure mode** | Discards class-relevant info if variance aligns with within-class direction | Requires $S_{W}$ to be invertible; fails for $N < d$ |

**Why LDA is preferred for medical image classification:**

1. **Label-aware:** Medical diagnosis is a *supervised* problem — radiologists provide labels (benign/malignant). LDA leverages this label information, while PCA ignores it.
2. **Separation, not variance:** A feature with low overall variance (e.g., a subtle texture biomarker) may be the *best* diagnostic discriminator. PCA would discard it; LDA would amplify it.
3. **Compact projection:** With $c$ classes, LDA produces at most $c-1$ meaningful axes, ideal for downstream classifiers.
4. **Empirical evidence:** In benchmark studies on lung-nodule CT, breast mammography, and brain MRI datasets, LDA-based features consistently outperform PCA-based features in classification accuracy (often by 5–15%).

---

#### (b) Explain the limitations of classical LDA in medical image processing. Briefly state how Regularised LDA and Kernel LDA address these limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Limitations of Classical LDA:**

1. **Small Sample Size (SSS) Problem:** When the number of medical images $N$ is much smaller than the feature dimension $d$ (very common in MRI voxels), $S_{W}$ becomes **singular** and $S_{W}^{-1}$ cannot be computed.

2. **Linearity Assumption:** LDA finds only *linear* decision boundaries. Many medical conditions have non-linear feature distributions (e.g., complex tumour morphologies).

3. **Gaussian & Equal-Covariance Assumption:** LDA is optimal under homoscedastic Gaussian class-conditional distributions. Real medical data may be multi-modal, skewed, or have class-dependent covariances.

4. **Rank Limitation:** At most $c-1$ discriminant directions are produced, which may be insufficient for highly heterogeneous diseases (e.g., multi-subtype cancer).

5. **Sensitivity to Outliers:** A few anomalous scans can severely distort $\mu_{i}$ and $S_{W}$.

**Regularised LDA (RLDA):** Add a regularisation term to stabilise $S_{W}$:

$$ S_{W}^{\text{reg}} \;=\; (1 - \alpha)\, S_{W} + \alpha\, I $$

with $\alpha \in (0, 1)$ tuned via cross-validation. This guarantees invertibility and improves robustness when $N \ll d$.

**Kernel LDA (KDA):** Implicitly map features to a high-dimensional Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space (RKHS) via a non-linear kernel $\kappa(x, x')$, then perform LDA in that space. The "kernel trick" allows non-linear discriminant boundaries **without** explicit coordinate transformation, at the cost of higher computational complexity ($O(N^{3})$ for eigen-decomposition on the $N \times N$ kernel matrix).

**Other remedies:** 2D-LDA operates directly on image matrices; Subclass LDA addresses multi-modal distributions; Sparse LDA selects only the most discriminative features for high-dimensional genomic-imaging data.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU LDA Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract the global mean** in $S_{B}$. Students often write $S_{B} = \sum N_{i}\mu_{i}\mu_{i}^{T}$ instead of $\sum N_{i}(\mu_{i}-\mu)(\mu_{i}-\mu)^{T}$. This is a **2-mark deduction** in most valuation schemes.
> 2. **Confusing $S_{T}$ with $S_{W}$.** Remember: $S_{T} = S_{W} + S_{B}$. Using $S_{T}$ instead of $S_{W}$ in the denominator gives **PCA**, not LDA.
> 3. **Reporting the unnormalised eigenvector.** Always report the **unit-norm** direction, especially if downstream projection lengths are computed.
> 4. **Claiming the rank of $S_{B}$ is $d$.** It is at most $c-1$. This is a frequently-tested conceptual trap.
> 5. **Skipping the verification step** in numerical problems. Always state the eigenvalue $\lambda_{\max}$ and confirm $S_{B} w = \lambda S_{W} w$ holds — it earns an extra 1 mark.
> 6. **Confusing the two-class closed form** $w \propto S_{W}^{-1}(\mu_{1}-\mu_{2})$ with the multi-class form. The closed form holds **only** for $c = 2$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** LDA is a *supervised* linear projection technique that maximises the ratio of *between-class* to *within-class* scatter.
- **Core Matrices:**
  - $S_{W} = \sum_{i} \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}_{i}} (x-\mu_{i})(x-\mu_{i})^{T}$
  - $S_{B} = \sum_{i} N_{i}(\mu_{i}-\mu)(\mu_{i}-\mu)^{T}$
  - $S_{T} = S_{W} + S_{B}$
- **Objective:** Maximise $J(w) = \dfrac{w^{T} S_{B} w}{w^{T} S_{W} w}$.
- **Solution:** Generalised eigenvalue problem $S_{B} w = \lambda S_{W} w$.
- **2-class closed form:** $w^{*} \propto S_{W}^{-1}(\mu_{1}-\mu_{2})$.
- **Multi-class:** At most $c-1$ discriminant vectors (rank of $S_{B}$).
- **Max value of $J$:** $J^{*}(w^{*}) = \lambda_{\max}$.
- **PCA vs. LDA:** PCA maximises total variance; LDA maximises class separability. LDA is **supervised**; PCA is **unsupervised**.
- **Limitations of LDA:** Singularity of $S_{W}$ when $N < d$; linear decision boundary; sensitive to outliers.
- **Remedies:** Regularised LDA (add $\alpha I$); Kernel LDA (non-linear extension); 2D-LDA (operates on image matrices).
- **Medical Applications:** Tumour classification, Alzheimer's detection, lung-nodule CAD, retinal disease grading, cytology screening.
- **Exam Tip:** Always **define** $\mu$, $\mu_{i}$ *before* using them; always **normalise** $w$ before projecting.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) Classifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT604 - MEDICAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) Classifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) Classifier** is a non-parametric, instance-based (lazy-learning) supervised classification algorithm that assigns an unknown medical image (query sample) to a class label based on a **majority vote** among its **K** closest training samples in the feature space, where "closeness" is quantified through a predefined distance metric.

In the context of **PBEBT604 – Medical Image Processing**, the KNN classifier is used to categorize pre-processed and feature-extracted medical images (e.g., classifying a tumor region as *malignant* or *benign*, or identifying tissue types in histopathology slides) by comparing the feature vector of an unknown image with the feature vectors of already-labelled training images.

### 1.2 Key Characteristics

- **Non-parametric** – makes no underlying assumption about the probability distribution of the data.
- **Instance-based / Lazy learner** – it does not build an explicit model during training; computation is deferred until classification time.
- **Supervised** – requires a labelled training set of medical images.
- **Memory-based** – the entire training dataset must be stored and used at inference time.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – The Junior Doctor & The Senior Panel:**
