<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

Define **hybrid imaging** in medical image processing. Name **two** clinical hybrid imaging systems currently in use.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Hybrid imaging is a diagnostic imaging technique that combines two or more imaging modalities — typically one structural (CT/MRI) and one functional (PET/SPECT) — into a single integrated scanner. The goal is to simultaneously obtain anatomical and physiological/metabolic information about the same region of the body, which is then registered and fused to produce a composite diagnostic image with superior clinical utility.
- **Two examples (1 mark):**
  1. **PET-CT** (Positron Emission Tomography – Computed Tomography): Used in oncology for tumor localization and staging.
  2. **SPECT-CT** (Single Photon Emission Computed Tomography – Computed Tomography): Used in cardiac perfusion imaging and bone scintigraphy.
- (Other valid examples: PET-MRI, fMRI-MRI fusion.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

Differentiate between **image registration** and **image fusion**. State **one** example of each in clinical practice.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
| Parameter | Image Registration | Image Fusion |
|---|---|---|
| **Operational Goal** | Geometrically align two or more images into a common coordinate system | Combine complementary information from aligned images into a single image |
| **Output** | A transformation matrix + aligned image set | A single composite image |
| **Stage in Pipeline** | Pre-processing step | Post-registration processing step |
| **Clinical Example** | Aligning pre-op MRI with intra-op US for brain surgery | PET-CT fusion for lung cancer staging |
| **Dependency** | Required **before** fusion | **Requires** successful registration as input |

- **Example of Registration:** Aligning a patient's diagnostic CT scan with a pre-operative MRI scan for neurosurgical planning (1 mark).
- **Example of Fusion:** Combining functional PET (FDG uptake) with anatomical CT (tissue density) to localize hypermetabolic tumor regions (1 mark).

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **three levels of image fusion** in detail. For each level, state the corresponding fusion technique and clinical scenario where it is most applicable.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT)** based image fusion process. State the fusion rule used for the approximation sub-band and the detail sub-bands. Justify why the DWT method is preferred over simple averaging in clinical hybrid imaging.

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Three Levels of Image Fusion [7 Marks]

**Level 1: Pixel-Level Fusion (2.5 marks)**
- Operates directly on raw pixel intensity values of source images.
- Requires **pre-registered** images.
- Techniques: Weighted averaging, Laplacian pyramid, Wavelet-based fusion, Brovey transform.
- **Clinical scenario:** Real-time fusion in image-guided surgery where computational speed is critical.

**Level 2: Feature-Level Fusion (2.5 marks)**
- Extracts salient features (edges, corners, textures, regions of interest) from each input image first.
- Fusion occurs on the **feature representation**, not the raw pixels.
- Techniques: Edge map fusion, Gabor wavelet feature fusion, segmentation-based fusion.
- **Clinical scenario:** Multi-modality tumor segmentation where anatomical and metabolic features must be jointly classified.

**Level 3: Decision-Level Fusion (2 marks)**
- Each input image is processed **independently** by a classifier/detector.
- Final fusion combines the **decisions** (e.g., probabilities, class labels) using voting, Bayesian, or Dempster-Shafer theory.
- **Clinical scenario:** Computer-Aided Detection (CAD) systems for mammography where multiple feature extractors vote on a tumor's presence.

#### Part (b) — DWT-Based Image Fusion [7 Marks]

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CT Image]:::a --> B1[DWT]:::d
    C[PET Image]:::a --> B2[DWT]:::d
    B1 --> B1a[LL1]:::sub
    B1 --> B1b[LH1]:::sub
    B1 --> B1c[HL1]:::sub
    B1 --> B1d[HH1]:::sub
    B2 --> B2a[LL2]:::sub
    B2 --> B2b[LH2]:::sub
    B2 --> B2c[HL2]:::sub
    B2 --> B2d[HH2]:::sub
    B1a --> R1[Average Rule]:::r
    B2a --> R1
    R1 --> LLa[Fused LL]:::sub
    B1b --> R2[Max Abs Rule]:::r
    B1c --> R2
    B1d --> R2
    B2b --> R2
    B2c --> R2
    B2d --> R2
    R2 --> Da[Fused Details]:::sub
    LLa --> ID[IDWT]:::id
    Da --> ID
    ID --> OUT[Fused Image]:::out
    classDef a fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef d fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sub fill:#FAFAD2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef r fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef id fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef out fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Step-by-Step Process (2.5 marks):**

**Step 1:** Apply 2D-DWT to both registered images (CT and PET). The DWT uses a low-pass filter (Lo_D) and a high-pass filter (Hi_D), followed by downsampling by 2, to produce four sub-bands: LL, LH, HL, HH.

**Step 2:** Apply fusion rules:
- **For approximation (LL) sub-band:** Use **weighted average** (or simple arithmetic mean) to preserve overall intensity distribution.

$$LL_{fused}(x, y) = \frac{LL_1(x, y) + LL_2(x, y)}{2}$$

- **For detail sub-bands (LH, HL, HH):** Use **maximum absolute value** rule to preserve edges, contours, and sharp metabolic activity spikes from PET.

$$D_{fused}(x, y) = \begin{cases} D_1(x, y) & \text{if } \vert D_1(x, y) \vert \geq \vert D_2(x, y) \vert \\ D_2(x, y) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 3:** Apply Inverse DWT (IDWT) using reconstruction filters (Lo_R, Hi_R) and upsampling to obtain the fused image.

**Justification (1.5 marks):** DWT is preferred over simple averaging because:
1. It preserves **multi-resolution** information — sharp metabolic "hot spots" from PET are preserved at high-frequency sub-bands, while anatomical context from CT is preserved at low-frequency approximation.
2. It avoids the **contrast reduction** problem mathematically proven for weighted averaging.
3. It enables **selective fusion** of specific frequency bands, which is impossible in spatial averaging.
4. It is computationally efficient (linear complexity $O(MN \log_2 N)$).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Laplacian Pyramid-based image fusion** method. Show the mathematical formulation for pyramid construction and reconstruction.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **wavelet-based fusion** and **PCA-based fusion** for hybrid imaging. State **two quality metrics** used to evaluate the performance of an image fusion algorithm and write their mathematical definitions.

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Laplacian Pyramid Fusion [7 Marks]

**Conceptual Foundation (2 marks):**
The Laplacian pyramid is a multi-scale image representation in which each level captures the **band-pass** information at a different spatial frequency. It is constructed by first building a Gaussian pyramid, then computing the difference between successive levels.

**Step-by-Step Construction (3.5 marks):**

**Step 1: Gaussian Pyramid Construction**
For level $l$ in the Gaussian pyramid, apply a Gaussian low-pass filter $w(m, n)$ and downsample by 2:

$$G_l(x, y) = \sum_{m=-2}^{2} \sum_{n=-2}^{2} w(m, n) \cdot G_{l-1}(2x + m, 2y + n)$$

where $G_0 = I$ (original image) and the Gaussian kernel is a 5×5 separable kernel.

**Step 2: Laplacian Pyramid Computation**
Each Laplacian level is the difference between the current Gaussian level and the upsampled next Gaussian level:

$$L_l(x, y) = G_l(x, y) - 4 \cdot G_{l+1}\left(\frac{x+y}{2}, \frac{y+x}{2}\right) \quad \text{(with appropriate upsampling)}$$

More precisely, the upsampling operator $U$ is applied first:

$$L_l = G_l - U(G_{l+1})$$

**Step 3: Fusion Rule**
At each Laplacian level $l$, apply maximum-selection rule (or weighted average):

$$L_{fused,l}(x, y) = \max\left[\vert L_{1,l}(x, y) \vert, \vert L_{2,l}(x, y) \vert\right] \cdot \text{sign}(\text{selected})$$

**Step 4: Reconstruction**
Sum the fused Laplacian levels to obtain the final fused image:

$$I_{fused} = L_{fused,0} + U(L_{fused,1}) + U^2(L_{fused,2}) + \ldots + U^{N-1}(L_{fused,N-1})$$

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- Preserves sharp features (edges, metabolic hot spots) at multiple scales.
- Avoids the blocky artifacts of wavelet-based methods.
- Shift-invariant — suitable for medical imaging where misregistration can be costly.

#### Part (b) — Wavelet vs PCA Fusion + Quality Metrics [7 Marks]

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Wavelet-Based Fusion | PCA-Based Fusion |
|---|---|---|
| **Decomposition Basis** | Localized wavelet basis (Daubechies, Haar) | Global orthogonal eigenvectors of covariance matrix |
| **Spatial-Frequency Info** | Both (multi-resolution) | Global only |
| **Computational Cost** | $O(MN \log_2 N)$ | $O(MN^2)$ |
| **Detail Preservation** | Excellent | Moderate |
| **Shift-Invariance** | Lacking (addressed by DT-CWT) | Yes |
| **Clinical Suitability** | High (preserves sharp features) | Moderate |

**Quality Metric 1: Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR) (1.5 marks)**

$$\text{PSNR} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{L^2}{\text{MSE}}\right)$$

where $L$ is the maximum possible pixel value (255 for 8-bit) and MSE is the Mean Squared Error between the fused and reference images:

$$\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{MN} \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} \left[I_{ref}(x,y) - I_{fused}(x,y)\right]^2$$

**Quality Metric 2: Mutual Information (MI) (1.5 marks)**

$$\text{MI}(I_1, I_2) = \sum_{a \in I_1} \sum_{b \in I_2} p(a, b) \cdot \log_2\left(\frac{p(a,b)}{p_1(a) \cdot p_2(b)}\right)$$

where $p(a,b)$ is the joint probability distribution of pixel intensities, and $p_1(a), p_2(b)$ are the marginal distributions. Higher MI indicates better information transfer from source images to the fused image.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Exam:**
> 1. **Confusing registration with fusion** — Registration is geometric alignment; fusion is information integration. Examiners deduct **1 mark** if these are used interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the constraint $w_1 + w_2 = 1$** in weighted averaging — examiners explicitly test this in 3-mark sub-questions.
> 3. **Stating only "averaging" without specifying maximum-selection** for detail sub-bands in DWT — you lose **2 marks** if the rule is not explicitly named.
> 4. **Drawing the DWT block diagram without 4 sub-bands** (LL, LH, HL, HH) — the diagram must show all **four** outputs.
> 5. **Forgetting the units of PSNR (dB)** and **MI (bits)** — minor but consistent deduction.
> 6. **Not mentioning the pre-requisite of registration** before fusion — examiners will specifically look for this logical ordering.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Image Fusion Definition:** Combining complementary information from two or more registered medical images into a single composite image. **[1 mark]**
- **Hybrid Imaging Examples:** PET-CT, SPECT-CT, PET-MRI. **[1 mark]**
- **Three Fusion Levels:** Pixel-level, Feature-level, Decision-level. **[2 marks]**
- **Registration vs Fusion:** Registration is a pre-requisite; fusion follows it. **[1 mark]**
- **Weighted Averaging Formula:** $I_{fused} = w_1 I_1 + w_2 I_2$ with $w_1 + w_2 = 1$. **[2 marks]**
- **Laplacian Pyramid:** Multi-scale band-pass representation; uses Gaussian pyramid + differences. **[2 marks]**
- **DWT Fusion:** Decompose into LL, LH, HL, HH; LL → average, details → max absolute value. **[3 marks]**
- **PSNR Formula:** $\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}(L^2 / \text{MSE})$, in dB. **[2 marks]**
- **MI Formula:** $\text{MI}(I_1, I_2) = \sum \sum p(a,b) \log_2[p(a,b) / (p_1(a) p_2(b))]$, in bits. **[2 marks]**
- **Contrast Reduction in Averaging:** Mathematically proven — fused variance $\leq$ convex combination of input variances. **[2 marks]**
- **DT-CWT:** Address shift-invariance limitation of standard DWT. **[1 mark]**
- **PSNR Threshold for Diagnostic Quality:** > **30 dB**. **[1 mark]**
- **Clinical Most-Used Method:** DWT-based fusion. **[1 mark]**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always draw a **block diagram** for any fusion question worth ≥ **7 marks**. Examiners allocate **2-3 marks** specifically for the diagram. A well-labeled, four-sub-band DWT diagram is the **single highest-yield drawing** for this topic.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Super Resolution Imaging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT604 - MEDICAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Super Resolution Imaging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Super Resolution (SR) Imaging** in medical image processing refers to the class of signal processing and computational techniques used to reconstruct or generate a **High-Resolution (HR) image** from one or more observed **Low-Resolution (LR) images** of the same scene or anatomy. The fundamental objective is to overcome the inherent resolution limitations imposed by the imaging sensor, optical diffraction limit, patient motion, and acquisition time constraints, thereby producing a diagnostically superior image with enhanced spatial detail.

In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme context, Super Resolution is defined under the umbrella of **Image Restoration and Reconstruction** as a special case of **ill-posed inverse problem**, where the goal is to invert the low-resolution degradation process to recover the high-frequency information that was lost or undersampled during acquisition.

The general observation model is expressed as:

$$Y_k = D_k B_k M_k X + N_k, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, N$$

where:
- $X$ is the desired high-resolution image (vectorized)
- $Y_k$ is the $k^{th}$ observed low-resolution image
- $M_k$ is the warping/geometric transformation matrix
- $B_k$ represents the blurring operator (point spread function)
- $D_k$ is the downsampling operator
- $N_k$ is the observation noise

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme explicitly categorizes SR into **(a) Single Image Super Resolution (SISR)** and **(b) Multi-Image / Multi-Frame Super Resolution (MISR / MFSR)**. Students must understand both the classical reconstruction-based approaches (e.g., IBP, MAP, POCS) and the modern learning-based paradigms (e.g., SRCNN, GAN-based SR).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

**Analogy: The "Detective Zoom-In"**

Imagine a forensic detective at a crime scene. The original CCTV camera captured a blurry photograph of a suspect's face. The detective has only **one blurry image** (Single Image SR) — yet by understanding how human faces *should* look (sharp eyes, defined jawline, fine skin texture), the detective's trained brain fills in the missing high-frequency details to produce a recognizable "reconstructed" face.

Now imagine the detective has **multiple CCTV frames** taken at slightly different angles and moments (Multi-Image SR). The detective can combine subtle sub-pixel shifts between the frames — where the tip of an ear appears in frame 1 but shifts slightly in frame 2 — to mathematically extract hidden detail, much like a stereo view produces depth. This is precisely how SR algorithms exploit **sub-pixel shifts** to recover information beyond the native sensor resolution.

**Plain English Intuition:**
- **Resolution** = how many pixels you have. More pixels = more detail.
- Your MRI machine has a fixed sensor. You can't physically add more pixels.
- But mathematically, you can **infer** what those extra pixels *should* look like, by using:
  - Priors about how natural images behave (smooth regions, sharp edges)
  - Information from multiple shifted frames
  - Learned patterns from thousands of training images

> [!NOTE]
> **Why SR matters in Medicine:** A radiologist diagnosing microcalcifications in a mammogram, or a neurologist assessing hippocampal subfields in an MRI, needs spatial resolution below 0.5 mm. Achieving this *physically* requires longer scan times (patient discomfort, motion artifacts) or expensive hardware. **SR provides a computational, low-cost alternative**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

The quality of SR reconstruction is universally evaluated using:

| Metric | Symbol | Purpose | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio** | **PSNR** | Pixel-level fidelity | **dB** |
| **Structural Similarity Index** | **SSIM** | Perceptual structural quality | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| **Mean Squared Error** | **MSE** | Pixel-wise error magnitude | Pixel² |
| **Universal Quality Index** | **UQI** | Luminance, contrast, structure | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** Always report PSNR in **dB (decibels)**. Always specify the upscaling factor (e.g., ×2, ×4) when presenting SR results, as it directly affects all quality metrics.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Decomposition of Super Resolution

Super Resolution is best understood as a four-stage pipeline:

### Stage 1 — Image Acquisition & Registration
- Capture **N** low-resolution frames $Y_1, Y_2, \ldots, Y_N$ of the same anatomical region.
- The frames must contain **sub-pixel shifts** relative to one another (otherwise, no new information is introduced).
- **Registration** computes the geometric warping matrices $M_k$ that align the frames to a common reference grid.

### Stage 2 — Forward Degradation Modeling
The forward (degradation) process is mathematically inverted. Knowing how the HR image $X$ was *destroyed* to become $Y_k$ allows us to design the inverse.

### Stage 3 — Reconstruction (The Core of SR)
Algorithms attempt to estimate $\hat{X}$ such that when $\hat{X}$ is re-degraded, it matches the observed $Y_k$. This is solved via optimization:

$$\hat{X} = \arg\min_X \left[ \sum_{k=1}^{N} \rho(Y_k, D_k B_k M_k X) + \lambda R(X) \right]$$

where $\rho(\cdot)$ is a fidelity (data-consistency) term, $R(X)$ is a regularization term enforcing prior knowledge (smoothness, sparsity), and $\lambda$ balances the two.

### Stage 4 — Quality Evaluation
The reconstructed $\hat{X}$ is compared against a ground-truth HR image (in simulation studies) using PSNR, SSIM, and visual inspection by radiologists.

## 2.2 Taxonomy of SR Methods (High-Yield)

### A. Interpolation-Based Methods (Baseline, NOT true SR)
- **Nearest Neighbor** — fastest, blocky artifacts
- **Bilinear** — averages 4 neighbors, smooth but blurry
- **Bicubic** — averages 16 neighbors using cubic polynomials, sharper than bilinear
- **Lanczos** — windowed sinc interpolation, preserves edges better

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction:** Interpolation methods are **NOT considered true super resolution** by the KTU board. They merely "guess" missing pixels using neighboring values — they do not recover new high-frequency information. They are the **baseline** against which true SR methods are compared.

### B. Reconstruction-Based Methods (Classical SR)
These methods explicitly invert the degradation model. The three most important are:

**1. Iterative Back-Projection (IBP)**
- Projects the reconstructed HR estimate back into the LR observation space, computes the error, and back-projects the error to refine the HR estimate.
- Iterative, simple, but lacks a unique solution (ill-posed).

**2. Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)**
- Bayesian framework: maximize the posterior probability $P(X \vert Y)$.
- Incorporates explicit image priors (e.g., Gaussian, Laplacian, Huber MRF).
- Provides a unique, stable solution.

**3. Projection Onto Convex Sets (POCS)**
- Defines multiple convex constraint sets (e.g., data consistency, bounded intensity, smoothness).
- Iteratively projects the estimate onto the intersection of these sets.
- Highly flexible, can incorporate sensor-specific priors.

### C. Learning-Based Methods (Modern SR)
- **Example-Based / Sparse Coding** (Yang et al., 2010) — learn LR–HR patch dictionaries
- **SRCNN** (Dong et al., 2014) — first deep CNN for SR: 3 layers (patch extraction, non-linear mapping, reconstruction)
- **FSRCNN** — faster, no bicubic pre-upsampling
- **SRGAN / ESRGAN** — adversarial training for perceptually realistic textures
- **Medical-specific SR**: cascaded 3D CNNs for MRI volume SR, GANs for CT denoising-and-SR

### D. Frequency Domain Methods
- **Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) + Inverse DWT**
- Aliasing in low-resolution images contains hidden high-frequency information that can be extracted via frequency-domain analysis.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Observation Model** | $Y_k = D_k B_k M_k X + N_k$ | Vectorized, $k=1,\ldots,N$ |
| 2 | **MSE** | $MSE = \frac{1}{MN} \sum_{i,j} (X_{ij} - \hat{X}_{ij})^2$ | $M \times N$ image size |
| 3 | **PSNR** | $PSNR = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{L^2}{MSE}\right)$ | $L$ = max pixel value (**255** for 8-bit) |
| 4 | **SSIM** | $SSIM = \frac{(2\mu_x \mu_{\hat{x}} + C_1)(2\sigma_{x\hat{x}} + C_2)}{(\mu_x^2 + \mu_{\hat{x}}^2 + C_1)(\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_{\hat{x}}^2 + C_2)}$ | $C_1=(0.01L)^2$, $C_2=(0.03L)^2$ |
| 5 | **IBP Update** | $\hat{X}^{n+1} = \hat{X}^{n} + \sum_k M_k^{-1} B_k^{-1} D_k^{-1} (Y_k - D_k B_k M_k \hat{X}^{n})$ | Iterative, back-projects residual |
| 6 | **MAP Objective** | $\hat{X}_{MAP} = \arg\max_X P(Y \vert X) P(X) = \arg\min_X \left[ -\log P(Y \vert X) - \log P(X) \right]$ | Bayesian framework |
| 7 | **Bicubic Kernel** | $W(x) = \begin{cases} (a+2)\vert x \vert^3 - (a+3)\vert x \vert^2 + 1 & \vert x \vert \leq 1 \\ a\vert x \vert^3 - 5a\vert x \vert^2 + 8a\vert x \vert - 4a & 1 < \vert x \vert < 2 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | $a = -0.5$ (commonly) |
| 8 | **Upscaling Factor** | $r = \frac{\text{HR dimension}}{\text{LR dimension}}$ | e.g., $r=2$ means ×2 |
| 9 | **PSNR Band (typical SR)** | $28 \text{ dB} \leq PSNR \leq 38 \text{ dB}$ | $\times 4$ on natural images |
| 10 | **SSIM Band (typical SR)** | $0.75 \leq SSIM \leq 0.95$ | Higher is better, max = 1 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** Never confuse **PSNR with SNR**. PSNR uses $L^2$ (peak intensity squared), not signal power. Mixing them is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.

## 2.4 Real-World Medical Engineering Utility

- **MRI Acceleration**: Compressed Sensing + SR reconstructs diagnostic-quality images from **k-space undersampled acquisitions** (e.g., 4× faster scans).
- **CT Dose Reduction**: Low-dose CT acquisitions are SR-enhanced to match full-dose image quality, reducing patient radiation exposure by **up to 70%**.
- **Ultrasound Spatial Compounding**: Multiple beam-steered LR frames are SR-fused to overcome diffraction-limited lateral resolution.
- **Digital Pathology**: Whole-slide histopathology images are reconstructed at gigapixel resolution from scanner-limited acquisitions.
- **Retinal Imaging**: OCT (Optical Coherence Tomography) employs SR to visualize individual cone photoreceptors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Bicubic Interpolation Kernel

Bicubic interpolation is the **de-facto baseline** for SR evaluation. It is a separable 2D extension of 1D cubic convolution.

### Step 1 — Start with the 1D Cubic Convolution

Cubic convolution approximates the ideal sinc interpolant using a piecewise cubic polynomial. For any real-valued coordinate $x$ within the support $[-2, +2]$:

$$W(x) = (a+2)\vert x \vert^3 - (a+3)\vert x \vert^2 + 1, \quad \text{for } 0 \leq \vert x \vert \leq 1$$

$$W(x) = a\vert x \vert^3 - 5a\vert x \vert^2 + 8a\vert x \vert - 4a, \quad \text{for } 1 < \vert x \vert < 2$$

$$W(x) = 0, \quad \text{otherwise}$$

Here, $a$ is a tunable sharpness parameter. The most widely accepted value is $a = -0.5$ (Keys' cubic convolution), which approximates a Catmull–Rom spline and minimizes interpolation bias.

### Step 2 — 2D Bicubic via Separability

The 2D bicubic kernel is the product of two 1D kernels (separable property):

$$W_{2D}(x, y) = W(x) \cdot W(y)$$

This means the 2D interpolation can be implemented as two sequential 1D passes (one along rows, one along columns), reducing computational cost from $O(N^2)$ to $O(2N)$ per output pixel.

### Step 3 — Evaluating the Output Pixel

For a target output coordinate $(u, v)$ falling between LR pixel grid points, the bicubic interpolated value is:

$$I_{HR}(u, v) = \sum_{i=-1}^{2} \sum_{j=-1}^{2} I_{LR}(x_i, y_j) \cdot W(u - x_i) \cdot W(v - y_j)$$

This involves a $4 \times 4 = 16$ neighborhood of LR pixels surrounding the target location.

## 3.2 Derivation: PSNR from MSE

**Step 1 — Define Mean Squared Error (MSE)**

For an HR ground-truth image $X$ of size $M \times N$ and its reconstruction $\hat{X}$:

$$MSE = \frac{1}{MN} \sum_{i=1}^{M} \sum_{j=1}^{N} (X_{i,j} - \hat{X}_{i,j})^2$$

**Step 2 — Relate PSNR to Signal-to-Noise Ratio**

The Signal-to-Noise Ratio (in dB) is:

$$SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{\text{Signal Power}}{\text{Noise Power}} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Peak Power for Signal Power**

In PSNR, the signal power is replaced by the **square of the peak possible pixel value** $L$ (since we want the ratio of peak signal to noise):

$$PSNR = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{L^2}{MSE} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Use Natural Logarithm Identity (alternative form)**

Using $\log_{10}(x) = \ln(x) / \ln(10)$:

$$PSNR = \frac{10}{\ln 10} \cdot \ln \left( \frac{L^2}{MSE} \right) \approx 4.3429 \cdot \ln \left( \frac{L^2}{MSE} \right) \text{ dB}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example (worked)**

Let $M = N = 256$, $L = 255$, and let the sum of squared errors $\sum (X - \hat{X})^2 = 102,400$.

$$MSE = \frac{102{,}400}{256 \times 256} = \frac{102{,}400}{65{,}536} \approx 1.5625$$

$$PSNR = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{255^2}{1.5625} \right) = 10 \log_{10}(41{,}616)$$

$$PSNR \approx 10 \times 4.6193 \approx 46.19 \text{ dB}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: IBP Update Rule

The Iterative Back-Projection algorithm inverts the degradation operator by projecting residuals.

**Step 1 — Define the Forward Operator**

The forward projection of an HR estimate $\hat{X}^n$ into the $k^{th}$ LR observation space is:

$$Y_k^{n} = D_k B_k M_k \hat{X}^n$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Observation Residual**

The error between the actual and projected LR image is:

$$E_k^n = Y_k - Y_k^n = Y_k - D_k B_k M_k \hat{X}^n$$

**Step 3 — Back-Project the Residual**

The back-projection operator is the (pseudo-)inverse of the forward operator. It maps the LR residual back into HR space:

$$\Delta \hat{X}^n = \sum_{k=1}^{N} M_k^{-1} B_k^{-1} D_k^{-1} E_k^n$$

**Step 4 — Update the HR Estimate**

$$\hat{X}^{n+1} = \hat{X}^n + \alpha \cdot \Delta \hat{X}^n$$

where $\alpha$ is a step-size (learning rate) parameter, typically $0 < \alpha \leq 1$.

**Step 5 — Convergence Criterion**

Iterate until either a maximum iteration count $N_{max}$ is reached, or:

$$\frac{\|\hat{X}^{n+1} - \hat{X}^n\|_2}{\|\hat{X}^n\|_2} < \epsilon$$

where $\epsilon$ is a small tolerance (e.g., $10^{-4}$).

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Method | Type | Data Requirement | Computation | Reconstruction Quality | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Nearest Neighbor** | Interpolation | 1 LR image | Very Low | Poor (blocky) | Baseline only |
| **Bicubic** | Interpolation | 1 LR image | Low | Moderate (smooth) | **Standard baseline** |
| **IBP** | Reconstruction | ≥2 LR images | Medium | Good | **Frequently asked** |
| **MAP** | Reconstruction | ≥1 LR image | Medium-High | Very Good | **Frequently asked** |
| **POCS** | Reconstruction | ≥1 LR image | Medium-High | Very Good | **Important** |
| **SRCNN** | Learning (CNN) | Training dataset | High (GPU) | Excellent | **Modern paradigm** |
| **SRGAN** | Learning (GAN) | Training dataset | Very High | Perceptually best | **Advanced** |

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example: Compute SSIM for a 2×2 Patch

Let a 2×2 HR ground-truth patch be $X = \begin{pmatrix} 120 & 130 \\ 125 & 128 \end{pmatrix}$ and the reconstructed patch be $\hat{X} = \begin{pmatrix} 122 & 128 \\ 124 & 130 \end{pmatrix}$.

**Step 1 — Compute Means**

$$\mu_X = \frac{120+130+125+128}{4} = 125.75, \quad \mu_{\hat{X}} = \frac{122+128+124+130}{4} = 126$$

**Step 2 — Compute Variances**

$$\sigma_X^2 = \frac{(120-125.75)^2 + (130-125.75)^2 + (125-125.75)^2 + (128-125.75)^2}{4}$$

$$= \frac{33.0625 + 18.0625 + 0.5625 + 5.0625}{4} = \frac{46.75}{4} = 11.6875$$

$$\sigma_{\hat{X}}^2 = \frac{(122-126)^2 + (128-126)^2 + (124-126)^2 + (130-126)^2}{4} = \frac{16+4+4+16}{4} = 10$$

**Step 3 — Compute Cross-Covariance**

$$\sigma_{X\hat{X}} = \frac{(120-125.75)(122-126) + (130-125.75)(128-126) + (125-125.75)(124-126) + (128-125.75)(130-126)}{4}$$

$$= \frac{(-5.75)(-4) + (4.25)(2) + (-0.75)(-2) + (2.25)(4)}{4} = \frac{23 + 8.5 + 1.5 + 9}{4} = \frac{42}{4} = 10.5$$

**Step 4 — Insert into SSIM Formula with L = 255**

$$C_1 = (0.01 \times 255)^2 = 6.5025, \quad C_2 = (0.03 \times 255)^2 = 58.5225$$

$$SSIM = \frac{(2 \cdot 125.75 \cdot 126 + 6.5025)(2 \cdot 10.5 + 58.5225)}{(125.75^2 + 126^2 + 6.5025)(11.6875 + 10 + 58.5225)}$$

$$= \frac{(31689 + 6.5025)(21 + 58.5225)}{(15813.0625 + 15876 + 6.5025)(80.21)}$$

$$= \frac{(31695.5025)(79.5225)}{(31695.5625)(80.21)} \approx 0.991$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** An SSIM of 0.991 indicates near-perfect structural preservation, consistent with the small pixel-wise errors in the patch.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Super Resolution Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete Super Resolution reconstruction pipeline, from low-resolution acquisition to high-resolution output with quality evaluation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HR Ground Truth X] --> B[Geometric Warp M_k]
    B --> C[Blurring B_k PSF]
    C --> D[Downsampling D_k]
    D --> E[Additive Noise N_k]
    E --> F[Observed LR Image Y_k]
    F --> G[Pre-processing: Denoising and Deblurring]
    G --> H[Image Registration to Compute M_k]
    H --> I[SR Reconstruction Engine]
    I --> J[IBP Module]
    I --> K[MAP Optimizer]
    I --> L[POCS Projector]
    I --> M[Deep CNN SRCNN or SRGAN]
    J --> N[HR Estimate X hat]
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Quality Evaluation]
    O --> P[PSNR Computation]
    O --> Q[SSIM Computation]
    O --> R[Visual Inspection by Radiologist]
    P --> S[Final Output HR Image]
    Q --> S
    R --> S
```

## 4.2 Sub-Pixel Shift Exploitation Diagram

The most fundamental principle of Multi-Image SR is the exploitation of sub-pixel shifts between LR frames. The diagram below shows how two shifted low-resolution images combine to yield higher effective sampling density.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LR_FRAME_1
        A1[Frame 1 Pixel Grid]
        A2[Sensor Cell A1 Captures Sample at x equals 0.0]
    end
    subgraph LR_FRAME_2
        B1[Frame 2 Pixel Grid Shifted by 0.5 Pixel]
        B2[Sensor Cell B1 Captures Sample at x equals 0.5]
    end
    A2 --> C[Combined Sampling Points at 0.0 and 0.5]
    B2 --> C
    C --> D[Effective HR Sampling Resolution Doubled]
    D --> E[Reconstruction via IBP or MAP]
    E --> F[Super Resolved HR Image]
```

## 4.3 Classical SR Algorithm Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Sequence of LR Images Y_1 to Y_N] --> B[Registration Module]
    B --> C[Estimate Motion Vectors M_k]
    C --> D[Choose SR Method]
    D --> E1[IBP Path]
    D --> E2[MAP Path]
    D --> E3[POCS Path]
    D --> E4[Deep Learning Path]
    E1 --> F1[Initialize X hat as Bicubic Upsampled]
    E2 --> F2[Define Likelihood and Prior]
    E3 --> F3[Define Convex Constraint Sets]
    E4 --> F4[Load Pre-trained Neural Network]
    F1 --> G1[Iterate Back Projection]
    F2 --> G2[Solve via Gradient Descent]
    F3 --> G3[Project onto Constraint Sets]
    F4 --> G4[Forward Pass Through Network]
    G1 --> H[HR Output X hat]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    H --> I[Post-processing: Edge Enhancement]
    I --> J[Final Diagnostic HR Image]
```

## 4.4 Learning-Based SR Workflow (SRCNN Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[LR Input Image] --> B[Bicubic Pre-upsampling to HR Size]
    B --> C[Patch Extraction Layer Conv 1 with 9x9 kernel ReLU]
    C --> D[Non-linear Mapping Layer Conv 2 with 5x5 kernel ReLU]
    D --> E[Reconstruction Layer Conv 3 with 5x5 kernel]
    E --> F[Output High Resolution Image]
    F --> G[Compute MSE Loss against Ground Truth HR]
    G --> H[Backpropagation Update Filters F1 F2 F3]
    H --> C
```

## 4.5 SR Method Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Need Super Resolution] --> B{Available Data}
    B -->|Single LR Image| C{SISR Path}
    B -->|Multiple LR Frames| D{MISR Path}
    C --> E{Real-time Required?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Bicubic or FSRCNN]
    E -->|No| G[SRCNN or ESRGAN]
    D --> H{Computational Budget}
    H -->|Low| I[IBP]
    H -->|Medium| J[MAP or POCS]
    H -->|High with Training Data| K[Deep Multi-frame Network]
    F --> L[Apply to Medical Image]
    G --> L
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip:** When answering KTU diagram-based questions, always **label each block with its mathematical operator** (e.g., label the blurring block as $B_k$, the downsampling block as $D_k$). Examiners reward students who connect the diagram to the equations.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Super Resolution Imaging. Differentiate between Single Image Super Resolution (SISR) and Multi-Image Super Resolution (MISR).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Super Resolution (SR) Imaging** is the process of reconstructing a high-resolution (HR) image from one or more observed low-resolution (LR) images by computationally inverting the degradation process, with the goal of recovering high-frequency spatial details lost during acquisition. (1 mark)

| Aspect | SISR | MISR |
|---|---|---|
| **Input** | Single LR image | Multiple LR images of the same scene |
| **Information source** | Internal image priors, learned patterns | Exploits sub-pixel shifts across frames |
| **Reconstruction quality** | Moderate to good | Typically superior to SISR |
| **Methods** | SRCNN, ESRGAN, sparse coding | IBP, MAP, POCS, multi-frame CNN |
| **Challenge** | Severely ill-posed, relies on strong priors | Requires precise image registration |

(2 marks for differentiation table)

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the role of registration in Multi-Image Super Resolution. What happens if registration is inaccurate?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Role of Registration (2 marks):**
Image registration estimates the geometric warping parameters $M_k$ that align each LR frame $Y_k$ to a common reference coordinate system. Accurate registration is **essential** for MISR because the algorithm relies on sub-pixel shifts between frames to reconstruct high-frequency content. Without precise registration, the back-projection step (IBP) or the data-consistency term (MAP, POCS) will project misaligned residuals back into HR space, causing severe ghosting and blur artifacts.

**Effect of Inaccurate Registration (1 mark):**
If registration error exceeds approximately 0.3–0.5 pixels, the reconstructed HR image exhibits:
- Edge ghosting and doubling
- Loss of fine detail
- Reduced PSNR by 3–6 dB
- Visible ringing artifacts near high-contrast boundaries (e.g., bone-soft tissue interfaces in CT)

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]

**a) [7 marks]** Derive the observation model for Multi-Image Super Resolution. Explain each operator ($M_k$, $B_k$, $D_k$, $N_k$) in detail with a block diagram.

**b) [7 marks]** With a neat flowchart, explain the Iterative Back-Projection (IBP) algorithm for SR reconstruction. Derive its update rule and discuss its convergence properties.

#### Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Set up the degradation model (2 marks)**

Let $X$ denote the desired HR image of size $L \times L$ (vectorized to a column vector of size $L^2 \times 1$). The $k^{th}$ LR observation $Y_k$ of size $M \times M$ ($M < L$) is generated by:

$$Y_k = D_k B_k M_k X + N_k$$

**Step 2 — Explain each operator (4 marks)**

| Operator | Mathematical Role | Physical Interpretation | Matrix Size |
|---|---|---|---|
| $M_k$ | Geometric warping | Models sub-pixel translation, rotation, and deformation between frames | $L^2 \times L^2$ |
| $B_k$ | Blurring | Represents the point spread function (PSF) of the imaging system — optical blur, motion blur | $L^2 \times L^2$ |
| $D_k$ | Downsampling | Integer decimation by factor $r = L/M$ in each dimension | $M^2 \times L^2$ |
| $N_k$ | Noise | Sensor noise, quantization noise, modeled as AWGN with variance $\sigma^2$ | $M^2 \times 1$ |

**Step 3 — Block diagram (1 mark)**

A block diagram in the form: $X \rightarrow [M_k] \rightarrow [B_k] \rightarrow [D_k] \rightarrow [+] N_k \rightarrow Y_k$, as shown in Section 4.1, should be drawn.

#### Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — IBP Concept (2 marks)**

Iterative Back-Projection was inspired by the algebraic reconstruction techniques used in CT. The idea is:
1. Start with an initial HR estimate $\hat{X}^0$ (typically bicubic upsampled LR image).
2. Simulate the LR observations from $\hat{X}^0$ using the forward model.
3. Compute the difference (residual) between simulated and actual LR images.
4. Back-project this residual into HR space to correct the estimate.
5. Repeat until convergence.

**Step 2 — IBP Update Rule Derivation (3 marks)**

The forward projection of $\hat{X}^n$ gives:

$$Y_k^{n} = D_k B_k M_k \hat{X}^n$$

The residual in LR space:

$$E_k^n = Y_k - Y_k^n = Y_k - D_k B_k M_k \hat{X}^n$$

Back-projection requires the pseudo-inverse of the forward operator. A simple choice is:

$$H_k = M_k^T B_k^T D_k^T$$

The total HR correction is the sum of back-projected residuals across all frames:

$$\Delta \hat{X}^n = \sum_{k=1}^{N} M_k^{-1} B_k^{-1} D_k^{-1} E_k^n$$

The HR estimate is updated:

$$\hat{X}^{n+1} = \hat{X}^n + \alpha \cdot \Delta \hat{X}^n$$

**Step 3 — Convergence Properties (2 marks)**

IBP **converges** in the least-squares sense under the following conditions:
- The forward operator must be well-conditioned (registration must be accurate).
- The step size $\alpha$ must be small enough to avoid divergence (typically $\alpha \leq 1$).
- A maximum of 20–50 iterations is usually sufficient.

**Drawbacks of IBP:**
- The solution is **not unique** — multiple HR images can produce the same LR observations (ill-posed).
- No explicit regularization is incorporated, leading to noise amplification.
- Convergence may be slow for high upscaling factors ($r > 4$).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice] [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]

**a) [7 marks]** Explain the Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) framework for Super Resolution. Derive the MAP cost function and discuss the role of the image prior $P(X)$.

**b) [7 marks]** With necessary equations, define PSNR and SSIM. Compute the PSNR for an image where $M = N = 512$, $L = 255$, and the sum of squared pixel errors is $2.5 \times 10^6$.

#### Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Bayesian Formulation (2 marks)**

In the Bayesian framework, the goal is to find the HR image $\hat{X}$ that maximizes the posterior probability given the observed LR images:

$$\hat{X}_{MAP} = \arg\max_X P(X \mid Y)$$

By Bayes' theorem:

$$P(X \mid Y) = \frac{P(Y \mid X) P(X)}{P(Y)}$$

Since $P(Y)$ is constant with respect to $X$, maximizing the posterior is equivalent to maximizing the product of the likelihood and the prior:

$$\hat{X}_{MAP} = \arg\max_X P(Y \mid X) \cdot P(X)$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Minimization via Negative Log-Likelihood (2 marks)**

Taking the negative logarithm (monotonic decreasing function) converts the product into a sum, which is numerically more stable:

$$\hat{X}_{MAP} = \arg\min_X \left[ -\log P(Y \mid X) - \log P(X) \right]$$

For Gaussian noise with variance $\sigma^2$, the likelihood term becomes:

$$-\log P(Y \mid X) = \frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \| Y_k - D_k B_k M_k X \|^2 + \text{const}$$

**Step 3 — Final MAP Cost Function with Regularization (2 marks)**

The image prior $P(X)$ is often chosen as a Markov Random Field (MRF) with Huber or Gaussian potential function. Substituting yields:

$$J(X) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \| Y_k - D_k B_k M_k X \|^2 + \lambda R(X)$$

The first term is the **data fidelity** (how well $\hat{X}$ explains the observed LR frames). The second term $R(X)$ is the **regularizer** (encourages smoothness, edge preservation, or sparsity in some transform domain). The parameter $\lambda$ balances the two — too small $\lambda$ leads to noisy reconstructions, too large $\lambda$ leads to over-smoothed images.

**Step 4 — Role of the Image Prior (1 mark)**

The image prior $P(X)$ is what makes the MAP problem **well-posed**, distinguishing it from the unregularized IBP approach. Common priors include:
- **Gaussian MRF**: $R(X) = \sum \| \nabla X \|^2$ — encourages smoothness
- **Laplacian / Total Variation**: $R(X) = \sum \| \nabla X \|_1$ — preserves sharp edges
- **Sparse coding priors**: assumes HR image patches are sparse in a learned dictionary

#### Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — PSNR Definition (2 marks)**

PSNR measures the pixel-level fidelity between the ground truth HR image $X$ and the reconstructed image $\hat{X}$:

$$PSNR = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{L^2}{MSE} \right) \text{ dB}$$

where $L$ is the peak pixel intensity (255 for 8-bit images).

**Step 2 — SSIM Definition (3 marks)**

SSIM is a perceptual metric that compares three local statistics — luminance, contrast, and structure:

$$SSIM(x, \hat{x}) = \frac{(2\mu_x \mu_{\hat{x}} + C_1)(2\sigma_{x\hat{x}} + C_2)}{(\mu_x^2 + \mu_{\hat{x}}^2 + C_1)(\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_{\hat{x}}^2 + C_2)}$$

where:
- $\mu_x$, $\mu_{\hat{x}}$: local means
- $\sigma_x^2$, $\sigma_{\hat{x}}^2$: local variances
- $\sigma_{x\hat{x}}$: local cross-covariance
- $C_1 = (K_1 L)^2$, $C_2 = (K_2 L)^2$ with $K_1 = 0.01$, $K_2 = 0.03$

SSIM ranges from $-1$ to $+1$, with $+1$ indicating perfect structural similarity.

**Step 3 — Numerical PSNR Computation (2 marks)**

Given: $M = N = 512$, $L = 255$, $\sum (X - \hat{X})^2 = 2.5 \times 10^6$.

$$MSE = \frac{2.5 \times 10^6}{512 \times 512} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^6}{262{,}144} \approx 9.5367$$

$$PSNR = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{255^2}{9.5367} \right) = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{65{,}025}{9.5367} \right)$$

$$= 10 \log_{10}(6819.6) \approx 10 \times 3.8337 \approx \mathbf{38.34 \text{ dB}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units**: Always report PSNR in **dB**. Simply writing "38.34" loses 0.5 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $L$ and signal power**: $L$ is the *peak* value, not the average. For 8-bit images, $L = 255$ always.
> 3. **Skipping the logarithm base**: $\log$ without subscript in engineering implies base 10. In mathematics, $\log$ implies base $e$. The KTU board expects base 10.
> 4. **Not stating the SSIM range**: Always mention that SSIM $\in [-1, +1]$ with $+1$ being perfect.
> 5. **Omitting the registration requirement in MISR**: Any MISR answer that does not mention image registration as a critical pre-processing step loses 2 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Super Resolution Imaging**

- ✅ **Definition**: SR reconstructs HR images from one (SISR) or more (MISR) observed LR images by inverting the degradation model.
- ✅ **Observation Model** (must memorize): $Y_k = D_k B_k M_k X + N_k$
- ✅ **Operators**: $M_k$ = warp, $B_k$ = blur, $D_k$ = downsample, $N_k$ = noise.
- ✅ **SR Taxonomy**: Interpolation (NOT true SR) → Reconstruction (IBP, MAP, POCS) → Learning (SRCNN, SRGAN).
- ✅ **Bicubic** is the **standard baseline** — all true SR methods are benchmarked against it.
- ✅ **PSNR formula**: $10 \log_{10}(L^2 / MSE)$ dB. Use $L = 255$ for 8-bit images.
- ✅ **SSIM range**: $-1$ to $+1$, with $+1$ being perfect. Composed of luminance × contrast × structure.
- ✅ **IBP update rule**: $\hat{X}^{n+1} = \hat{X}^n + \alpha \sum_k M_k^{-1} B_k^{-1} D_k^{-1}(Y_k - D_k B_k M_k \hat{X}^n)$
- ✅ **MAP objective**: $J(X) = \sum_k \| Y_k - D_k B_k M_k X \|^2 + \lambda R(X)$
- ✅ **Sub-pixel shifts** are the fundamental information source for MISR — without them, no new HR detail can be recovered.
- ✅ **Registration accuracy** below 0.3–0.5 pixels is mandatory for MISR to succeed.
- ✅ **Medical applications**: MRI acceleration, low-dose CT, ultrasound compounding, retinal OCT, digital pathology.
- ✅ **Typical PSNR range** for ×4 SR on natural images: **28–38 dB**. Higher is always better.
- ✅ **SRCNN** is the first deep CNN for SR with 3 layers: patch extraction → non-linear mapping → reconstruction.
- ✅ **Karnam**: (a) Always mention that SR is an **ill-posed inverse problem**. (b) Always state the upscaling factor $r$ when reporting results. (c) Always pair PSNR with SSIM — PSNR alone is insufficient for perceptual quality assessment.
